proposals.title: Closed-Loop Affective Robotics: Real-Time Behavioral Adaptation in a Simulated Therapy Context Using Personalized Perception Models
proposals.motivation: Socially assistive robots (SARs) for mental health, such as therapy for depression or anxiety, often rely on pre-programmed or scripted interactions, limiting their responsiveness to a user's dynamic affective state. Real-time, personalized adaptation based on continuous affective perception is a critical unsolved challenge for creating effective and engaging therapeutic agents. This work addresses the gap between offline affect recognition models and the need for closed-loop control that can modulate robot behavior to support therapeutic goals.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a personalized, mixed-effects random forest model for clinical depression severity prediction into a real-time robot control architecture will enable adaptive behavioral responses that are perceived as more empathetic and effective by users in a simulated therapy dialogue task, compared to a non-adaptive robot.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop system where a robot's behavioral policy (e.g., speech content, prosody, gesture, and pacing) is dynamically guided by a real-time affective perception module. This module will fuse multi-modal data (vocal acoustics, facial action units from video, and linguistic features) processed through a personalized Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) model, trained on prior student health study data to infer depression/anxiety severity. The robot's decision-making will be implemented using a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework, where the MERF output forms the observation model, and the reward function is designed to encourage therapeutic behaviors aligned with principles from Collaborative Online International Learning (COIL) and responsible neurotech design.
proposals.contribution: This project will contribute a novel, ethically-grounded architecture for closed-loop affective robotics, demonstrating a concrete integration of personalized machine learning for mental health into real-time agent control. It will provide empirical evidence on the feasibility and perceived impact of such adaptive systems, advancing the field of SARs beyond scripted interactions. The work will also yield a discussion framework for the neuroethical challenges of real-time affective AI in therapeutic settings.
proposals.novelty: While MERFs have been used for offline depression prediction, their integration as the core perception engine for real-time robot control is novel. The approach uniquely combines a personalized statistical model with a POMDP-based controller specifically designed for therapeutic interaction, moving beyond simple threshold-based reactivity to probabilistic, goal-directed adaptation.

proposals.title: Assessing the Sufficiency of a Single Accelerometer for Disentangling Vocalization-Related Motion Artifacts in Real-World Affective Computing
proposals.motivation: Wearable sensing for mental health, such as detecting depression and anxiety from vocal prosody, is promising but confounded by motion artifacts from head and jaw movements. Pre-2026 work often uses multiple sensors or assumes motion is negligible, limiting ecological validity and scalability. Determining if a single, minimally obtrusive accelerometer can adequately capture these complex motion dynamics is critical for developing robust, real-world affective monitoring systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A single accelerometer, when processed with appropriate non-linear models, can provide a sufficient motion reference to isolate and correct for head and jaw motion artifacts contaminating audio-based vocal biomarkers in naturalistic settings.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized audio and single-axis accelerometer data from the sternum during structured tasks (speaking, chewing, head turning) and free-living periods in a student cohort. Using pre-2026 methods, we will engineer motion features (e.g., spectral entropy, jerk) and apply Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF) to model the non-linear relationship between accelerometer signal and artifact-corrupted audio features (e.g., pitch, jitter). The model's ability to recover 'clean' vocal biomarkers will be validated against motion-captured ground truth in a lab subset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven evaluation of a low-cost motion artifact correction method, directly informing the design of scalable wearable systems for mental health. It will contribute a validated protocol and open dataset for benchmarking motion artifact handling in ecological affective computing research.
proposals.novelty: While multi-sensor motion reference is common, rigorously testing the lower bound of sufficiency—a single accelerometer—for complex orofacial artifacts in naturalistic audio is novel. The application of MERF allows personalization of artifact models, moving beyond population-level assumptions.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop Affective Computing Intervention System: An Ear-Worn Device for Real-Time Anxiety Modulation via Biofeedback and Auditory Stimuli
proposals.motivation: Current wearable sensing focuses on passive monitoring, creating a gap between detection and therapeutic action. This is critical in mental health, where timely intervention for anxiety can prevent escalation. An ear-worn form factor is ideal for continuous, socially acceptable wear and is proximal to neural pathways for auditory and vagal nerve stimulation, offering a novel intervention point.
proposals.hypothesis: That a closed-loop ear-worn device, which uses sensed physiological and vocal markers to trigger personalized auditory biofeedback or calming stimuli, can significantly reduce self-reported and physiologically measured anxiety states in ecologically valid settings compared to sensing-only monitoring.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype earring device integrating existing EDA/PPG sensors with a micro-speaker. A Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) model, trained on lab-induced and naturalistic anxiety data from student cohorts, will personalize the prediction of anxiety severity from sensor streams. Upon detecting elevated anxiety, a closed-loop controller will initiate an intervention: either (1) real-time sonification of heart rate to guide paced breathing, or (2) delivery of pre-selected, calming auditory stimuli. A 4-week ecological momentary assessment (EMA) study with students will compare intervention-active vs. sensing-only periods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for 'active' affective computing wearables that move beyond monitoring to embedded, context-aware intervention. It contributes a novel method for personalized, closed-loop anxiety modulation in daily life and generates crucial data on the efficacy and ethical design parameters for such neurotech, directly informing responsible innovation.
proposals.novelty: It inverts the primary function of a wellness wearable from passive sensing to autonomous, biofeedback-driven intervention. The integration of a MERF-based personalized trigger with a socially acceptable, ear-based delivery mechanism for real-time therapy is unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Personalized Adaptive Filtering for Motion Artifact Cancellation in Wearable Affect and Pain Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Wearable sensors for monitoring physiological signals like electrodermal activity (EDA) and photoplethysmography (PPG) are crucial for objective, longitudinal assessment of pain, anxiety, and depression. However, motion artifacts severely corrupt these signals in real-world settings, limiting the reliability of derived biomarkers. Pre-2026 methods often use generic noise models, failing to adapt to individual movement patterns and sensor placements, which degrades personalized health predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a personalized, adaptive noise cancellation algorithm, trained on individual-specific movement and physiological data, will significantly outperform generic methods in recovering clean physiological signals during free-living activity, leading to more accurate predictions of clinical states like pain and depression severity.
proposals.method: We will develop a mixed-effects random forest (MERF) framework for adaptive filtering, treating the noise-cancellation parameters as random effects personalized to each user. The model will fuse high-frequency accelerometer data with raw physiological signals (EDA, PPG) from wrist-worn devices. We will train and validate the algorithm on an existing longitudinal dataset of student participants, collected during daily activities and annotated with self-reported anxiety and mood states, using clean signal segments during rest as a reference.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, personalized signal processing pipeline that directly improves the fidelity of wearable data for affective computing. It will enable more robust extraction of vocalization-correlated and time-dependent physiological features for experience sampling. The resulting clean signals will enhance the performance of downstream models for predicting clinical depression severity and pain episodes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing motion artifact cancellation not as a generic signal processing task, but as a personalized machine learning problem using MERFs, explicitly modeling inter-individual variability in movement-physiology coupling—a method previously applied to prediction but not to low-level signal reconstruction for health sensing.

proposals.title: Multimodal Affective Computing for Co-Morbid Depression and Pain Assessment in Chronic Heart Failure
proposals.motivation: Chronic cardiovascular conditions like heart failure (HF) are strongly associated with high rates of co-morbid depression and chronic pain, which significantly worsen prognosis and quality of life. Pre-2026 affective computing research, including Picard's work, has shown promise in passively sensing depression and pain via vocal, physiological, and behavioral signals, but validation has been limited to controlled lab studies or general populations. There is a critical need to test these digital phenotyping systems in real-world clinical chronic disease cohorts where accurate, continuous monitoring could enable timely, personalized interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal system, integrating vocal acoustics, electrodermal activity, and movement data from a wearable device, will generate digital biomarkers that correlate more strongly with clinician-assessed depression severity and self-reported pain in HF patients than single-modality models or standard questionnaire-based assessments.
proposals.method: We will deploy a validated wearable sensor suite (e.g., Empatica E4) and a smartphone app for periodic vocal sampling to a cohort of 80 HF patients over a 4-week observational study. Using pre-2026 state-of-the-art methods, we will extract features including vocal prosody and spectral measures, skin conductance level and fluctuations, and actigraphy. We will apply Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) models, a core technique from Picard's recent work, to build personalized predictive models of depression (PHQ-8 scores) and pain (Brief Pain Inventory) that account for both population-level trends and individual-specific variations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous validation of a passive, multimodal affective computing system for monitoring psychosocial distress in a specific, high-need chronic disease population. It will contribute novel digital biomarkers for the co-occurrence of depression and pain in HF and demonstrate a practical framework for personalized, longitudinal assessment in clinical cardiology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the targeted application of MERF-based personalized modeling to the complex, multimodal sensor data stream from a chronic cardiovascular disease cohort, explicitly addressing the intertwined experiences of depression and pain, which are typically assessed separately.

proposals.title: Sensor Fusion for Context-Aware Affective Computing: Enhancing In-Ear Physiological Monitoring via Motion-Aware Kalman Filtering
proposals.motivation: Wearable earpieces show promise for continuous, unobtrusive monitoring of physiological signals (e.g., heart rate, vocal biomarkers) linked to pain, anxiety, and depression. However, motion artifacts severely degrade signal quality, limiting reliable inference in real-world settings. Robust motion context estimation and cancellation is a critical, unsolved challenge for translating lab-based affective computing models to daily life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fusing data from multiple low-power, in-ear sensors (e.g., accelerometer, gyroscope, barometer, and photoplethysmography) using techniques adapted from robotic state estimation will yield a more accurate and context-rich motion model, enabling superior artifact cancellation and more precise extraction of physiologically-relevant features.
proposals.method: We will adapt an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) framework, commonly used in robotics for sensor fusion and localization, to fuse inertial and barometric data from a custom research earpiece. This will create a high-fidelity estimate of head motion and activity context (e.g., walking, talking, resting). This motion state estimate will then be used to inform a multi-modal artifact cancellation algorithm (e.g., using adaptive filtering or a denoising autoencoder) to clean the physiological signals. The cleaned features will serve as input to mixed-effects random forest models for predicting depression and anxiety severity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, principled framework for motion-robust in-ear affective sensing. It will advance the field by providing a method to generate cleaner, context-labeled physiological datasets from naturalistic settings. This directly enables more personalized and accurate digital biomarkers for mental health, supporting Picard's research on depression and anxiety monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While sensor fusion is established in robotics, its application to in-ear affective computing for explicit motion context modeling and cancellation is novel. The approach moves beyond simple filtering by using a state estimation framework to derive semantically meaningful activity contexts that can inform both signal processing and subsequent behavioral analysis.

proposals.title: A Networked Multi-Site Body Sensor Array for Continuous, Context-Aware Modeling of Pain and Depression Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing research often relies on single-point sensors (e.g., a wristband) or sporadic lab measurements, which fail to capture the complex, distributed physiology of experiences like pain and depression. Pre-2026 work highlights the temporal dynamics of these states and the value of vocal and movement biomarkers, but lacks a scalable, unobtrusive method for continuous, multi-modal data fusion across the body in naturalistic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: That a synchronized network of earring-like sensors, deployed at multiple body sites (e.g., ears, wrists, sternum), will capture a richer, more discriminative set of motion and physiological features (e.g., gait, posture, heart rate variability, skin conductance) than single-point sensing, enabling more accurate and personalized prediction of clinical depression severity and pain episodes over time.
proposals.method: We will develop a hardware platform of 3-5 discreet, wireless sensor nodes, each containing an IMU, photoplethysmogram (PPG), and electrodermal activity (EDA) sensor. Data will be synchronized via a custom protocol and streamed to a smartphone. We will employ Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF), a method from Picard's prior work, to model both population-level trends and personalized deviations. Features will include cross-node correlation of movement, vocal prosody extracted from an ear-node microphone, and physiological coordination, analyzed within temporal windows to model state transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, wearable hardware platform for dense, networked physiological sensing, 2) a new MERF-based analytical framework for multi-stream, temporal data fusion, and 3) validated biomarkers for depression and pain derived from whole-body dynamics, advancing towards real-world digital phenotyping.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal sensing exists, the proposed approach is novel in its specific focus on a scalable, jewelry-like form factor deployed as a synchronized *network* across the body, and in its application of mixed-effects models to this new high-dimensional, multi-source data structure for mental health monitoring.

proposals.title: Quantifying Circadian Risk: Applying GARCH and Stochastic Volatility Models to Wearable Sleep Data for Predicting Depression and Anxiety Vulnerability
proposals.motivation: Circadian disruption is a well-established transdiagnostic risk factor for mood disorders like depression and anxiety, but current sleep regularity metrics (e.g., interdaily stability) are descriptive and lack a predictive framework for individual 'risk' of dysregulation. Pre-2026, financial econometrics has developed sophisticated models, like GARCH, to quantify and forecast volatility—the instability of a system over time—yet these have seen minimal application to physiological time-series data in mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that volatility metrics derived from financial time-series models, when applied to longitudinal sleep-wake data, will provide a superior predictive signal for impending increases in depression and anxiety severity compared to traditional sleep regularity and duration metrics.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-frequency sleep-wake data (from actigraphy or consumer wearables) and daily ecological momentary assessment (EMA) mood scores from existing student cohort studies. We will fit Generalized Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity (GARCH) and Stochastic Volatility models to time-series of daily sleep midpoint and duration to generate personalized, time-varying 'circadian volatility' indices. These indices, along with vocal biomarkers from phone sensors, will be used as features in Mixed Effects Random Forests (a method from Picard's prior work) to predict subsequent weekly changes in PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, quantifiable 'risk' metric for circadian disruption, directly linking temporal instability in behavior to clinical outcomes. It will provide a new methodological bridge between econometrics and digital phenotyping, enabling more proactive, personalized mental health monitoring and intervention timing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual translation of financial risk modeling to a physiological and behavioral domain, specifically framing sleep irregularity not just as a pattern but as a quantifiable volatility with predictive clinical utility, which is absent from pre-2026 sleep and affective computing literature.

proposals.title: Passive, Large-Scale Tracking of Sleep Regularity Index via Consumer Wearables for Early Detection of Depression and Anxiety Symptom Fluctuations
proposals.motivation: The Sleep Regularity Index (SRI) is a pre-2026 metric quantifying day-to-day consistency in sleep-wake timing, shown to be a stronger predictor of mental health outcomes than sleep duration alone. However, existing SRI studies are limited by small, short-term samples using research-grade actigraphy. The proliferation of consumer wearables (e.g., Fitbit, Apple Watch) offers an unprecedented opportunity for large-scale, passive, and longitudinal monitoring of SRI in real-world settings, aligning with Picard's focus on temporal patterns of experience and health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deviations in passively tracked SRI, derived from consumer wearable data, will significantly predict concurrent and near-future fluctuations in self-reported depression and anxiety severity in a large, heterogeneous population.
proposals.method: We will partner with a large-scale digital health cohort (e.g., All of Us Research Program) to access de-identified, multi-year sleep interval data from consumer wearables for >10,000 participants. SRI will be calculated daily using the pre-2026 formula. We will apply Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF), a method from Picard's recent work, to model the relationship between SRI time-series features (e.g., rolling mean, variance, sharp declines) and longitudinal mental health scores (PHQ-9, GAD-7), while accounting for individual random effects and covariates like age and gender. Personalized prediction models will be developed.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first validated pipeline for deriving a clinically meaningful sleep metric (SRI) from consumer wearables at population scale. It will provide novel evidence on the temporal dynamics linking sleep irregularity to mental health, moving beyond cross-sectional associations. The project will also demonstrate a responsible neurotech framework for large-scale passive monitoring with clear clinical utility.
proposals.novelty: While SRI is known, its measurement via consumer wearables and integration with MERF for personalized, longitudinal mental health prediction in thousands is novel. This scales Picard's work on personalized depression prediction into the ubiquitous, passive data domain, directly addressing the socio-technical challenge of deriving clinical insight from consumer neurotech.

proposals.title: Modeling the Temporal Dynamics of Co-Occurring Affective States: A Multitask Learning Framework for Mood, Stress, and Physiological Health
proposals.motivation: Prior affective computing research often treats mood, stress, and health metrics as independent prediction tasks, ignoring their complex, real-time interactions. This siloed approach limits the ecological validity and personalization of models for mental health monitoring, such as in depression and anxiety. A dynamic, integrated model is needed to better capture the holistic human experience as studied in Picard's work on pain, time, and vocalizations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a joint temporal model of mood, stress, and physiological health indicators will significantly outperform independent task models in predictive accuracy and personalization, by capturing their bidirectional causal and correlational dynamics.
proposals.method: We will employ a longitudinal study design with student participants, collecting multimodal data (vocal acoustics from daily audio snippets, wearable physiology, and ecological momentary assessments of mood/stress). The core technical approach is a novel Multitask Gaussian Process (MTGP) framework with coupled kernel functions, explicitly modeling temporal correlations and shared latent representations between the tasks. This will be compared against state-of-the-art independent models, including the Mixed Effects Random Forests Picard has previously used.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated computational framework for modeling interacting affective states over time, moving beyond single-task prediction. It will provide new insights into the temporal signatures of co-occurring symptoms relevant to depression and anxiety, advancing personalized digital health tools.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly relaxing the independence assumption within a temporal Bayesian framework, moving beyond simply running multiple models in parallel to modeling their dynamic interactions as a core part of the learning process, which is non-trivial given existing single-task focused literature.

proposals.title: Robust Remote Photoplethysmography and Affective State Inference from Partially Obscured Facial Videos
proposals.motivation: Remote measurement of physiology and affective states via facial video is crucial for scalable, unobtrusive health monitoring, particularly for conditions like depression and anxiety. However, real-world use cases, such as telehealth sessions where a patient may turn away, wear glasses, or rest their chin on a hand, often result in partial facial occlusion or non-frontal poses, degrading the reliability of existing computer vision and rPPG (remote photoplethysmography) methods. This limits the ecological validity and inclusivity of digital mental health tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-modal, attention-based deep learning architecture, trained on synchronized video, audio, and contextual data, can accurately infer heart rate variability (HRV) and self-reported affective states even when the face is partially obscured or in profile, by learning to weight visible facial regions and complementary vocal biomarkers.
proposals.method: We will collect a new dataset of controlled lab sessions with participants (e.g., university students) undergoing mild stressor tasks, where video (face often in profile/occluded), audio (vocalizations), and ECG ground truth are recorded alongside ecological momentary assessments of pain and mood. The core technical approach will be a hybrid neural network: a 3D CNN with a spatial attention mechanism (e.g., a modified Residual Attention Network) processes video patches to extract robust rPPG signals from visible regions, while a parallel temporal model (e.g., a LSTM) processes acoustic features (e.g., MFCCs, jitter) from audio. These streams will be fused using a transformer encoder to model temporal dependencies, with the final output predicting HRV and depression/anxiety severity scores via a mixed-effects random forest layer for personalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel multi-modal architecture for robust physiology extraction under realistic visual constraints, advancing affective computing for inclusive telehealth; 2) a new open dataset pairing occluded facial video with high-quality physiological and affective labels; and 3) a framework for responsible neurotech that addresses real-world socio-technical challenges of continuous, remote monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on frontal, clear-face rPPG or audio-only affect recognition, the novelty lies in explicitly modeling and compensating for visual occlusion via attention mechanisms and demonstrating that fused audio-visual-temporal models can maintain accuracy for both physiological and clinical severity prediction in non-ideal conditions, a critical step for practical deployment.

proposals.title: Personalized Physiological Baselines: A Mixed-Effects Forest Framework for Quantifying Individual Resting Autonomic Tone in Pain and Depression
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing and digital health models often use population-level physiological baselines, which fail to account for significant individual differences in resting autonomic tone. This limits the accuracy of personalized predictions for conditions like clinical depression and acute pain, where deviations from a personal baseline are more meaningful than absolute values. Establishing a robust, personalized 'rest condition' is therefore a critical prerequisite for precise, individualized mental and physical health monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a personalized physiological baseline, derived from multi-modal sensor data during a guided rest protocol, provides a more accurate and universal reference for quantifying subsequent affective and sensory experiences (e.g., pain, anxiety) than a population norm. This personalized baseline will significantly improve the prediction of depression severity and pain-related vocalizations.
proposals.method: We will collect multi-modal data (ECG, EDA, respiration, and short vocal audio clips) from a longitudinal student cohort during a standardized, guided rest condition. We will extend the Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) algorithm, previously used for depression, to model personalized resting autonomic tone as a latent random effect. This model will integrate features from all modalities to generate an individualized baseline vector for each participant. Subsequent experimental sessions involving controlled stress or pain induction will use deviations from this personalized baseline as primary features in predictive models of self-reported state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational method for deriving and validating personalized physiological baselines, moving the field beyond one-size-fits-all norms. It will contribute a new, open dataset linking personalized rest physiology to subsequent affective and sensory experiences. The framework will directly improve the personalization and accuracy of models for depression and pain monitoring, key themes in Picard's research.
proposals.novelty: While the concept of a rest baseline is not new, the application of a MERF framework to formally model inter-individual differences in resting autonomic tone as a foundational, transferable random effect across different affective states (depression and pain) is novel. This approach treats the personal baseline not as a simple average, but as a learned, hierarchical parameter that informs all subsequent within-person analyses.

proposals.title: Validating Ecological Stress Models: A Multi-Modal Comparison of Laboratory-Induced and Real-World Physiological and Vocal Signatures
proposals.motivation: Laboratory stress-induction paradigms (e.g., Trier Social Stress Test) are foundational for affective computing and mental health research, yet their ecological validity remains a critical, open question. Pre-2026, there is a pressing need to rigorously test if these controlled protocols elicit physiological and behavioral states that genuinely mirror the complex, sustained stressors of daily life, especially in populations like students vulnerable to anxiety and depression. Without such validation, interventions and digital biomarkers developed in the lab may fail to translate to real-world efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-modal feature set derived from wearable physiology and naturalistic vocalizations will reveal significant, generalizable similarities between acute laboratory-induced stress and ecologically sampled real-world stress episodes, thereby validating the laboratory model for specific affective states relevant to depression and anxiety.
proposals.method: We will recruit a cohort of university students, instrumenting them with Empatica E4 wristbands to capture electrodermal activity, heart rate, and temperature. Participants will undergo a standardized laboratory stress test and then provide continuous, real-world data over a two-week period, annotated via ecological momentary assessment for self-reported stress. We will extract high-frequency heart rate variability, skin conductance response, and prosodic vocal features (pitch, jitter, speech rate) from smartphone audio snippets. A Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) model, accounting for individual baselines, will be trained to classify stress states, with model performance and SHAP values used to compare feature importance across lab and field contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing the ecological validity of laboratory stress models, directly informing the design of future studies on depression and anxiety. It will contribute a novel, validated multi-modal feature set for personalized stress prediction that bridges lab and life, advancing the development of responsible, real-world neurotechnology and digital mental health tools.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares lab and field data separately, this proposal uniquely employs a personalized MERF architecture to directly model and compare the feature structures of stress across contexts, integrating objective physiology with naturalistic vocal biomarkers—a combination under-explored in pre-2026 ecological validation research.

proposals.title: Modeling Micro-Movements for Robust Remote Vocal Biomarker Assessment of Affective States
proposals.motivation: Remote measurement of vocal biomarkers for pain, depression, and anxiety offers scalable, passive mental health monitoring. However, even in controlled settings, small, involuntary head movements can introduce subtle acoustic distortions (e.g., spectral shifts, amplitude modulation) that confound signal analysis, potentially leading to misestimation of affective state severity. Addressing this artifact is critical for developing reliable, personalized predictive models like Mixed Effects Random Forests for clinical deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling and compensating for small head movements using synchronized motion and acoustic data will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of vocal biomarker extraction for predicting clinical depression and anxiety severity in controlled remote recordings.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized high-fidelity audio and head motion data (via a lightweight inertial measurement unit headband) from student participants during structured tasks designed to elicit affective vocalizations. A signal-processing pipeline will be developed to first characterize motion artifacts using a state-space model that maps motion kinematics to acoustic feature perturbations (e.g., MFCCs, pitch). Subsequently, we will implement a compensation algorithm, likely a variant of a Wiener filter or a deep learning architecture (e.g., a denoising autoencoder), to generate artifact-corrected vocal features. These cleaned features will be used as inputs to a Mixed Effects Random Forest model to predict depression/anxiety scores, comparing performance against a baseline model using uncorrected features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated methodological framework for motion-artifact compensation in remote vocal analysis, directly increasing the fidelity of digital biomarkers for pain and affective disorders. It will yield a publicly available dataset of synchronized motion and voice, enabling further research into embodied aspects of emotional expression. The results will inform the responsible development of neurotech and health sensing tools by addressing a key socio-technical challenge: ensuring data integrity for equitable and accurate personalization.
proposals.novelty: While motion artifact correction is standard in fields like EEG, its systematic application to the acoustic domain for affective computing is novel. This approach moves beyond simply dismissing artifacts as negligible to explicitly modeling them as a structured noise process, integrating principles from sensor fusion and personalized machine learning common in Picard's prior work.

proposals.title: Remote Multimodal Fusion of Vocal, Thermal, and Ocular Signals for Continuous Assessment of Stress and Depressive Symptom Severity
proposals.motivation: Remote, passive sensing of mental health states is critical for scalable, ecologically valid monitoring, especially for conditions like depression and anxiety. Pre-2026 work primarily relies on vocal acoustics or wearable physiology, but remote, non-contact signals like facial skin temperature (via thermal imaging) and pupillary dynamics (via webcam) remain underexplored for their combined predictive power in naturalistic settings. This gap limits the robustness and personalization of digital mental health tools.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multimodal model fusing remotely acquired vocal, facial thermal, and pupillometry signals will significantly outperform models using any single modality in predicting self-reported stress and depression severity scores, and that these signals will reveal distinct, complementary physiological signatures of affective states.
proposals.method: We will collect a longitudinal dataset from student participants during remote study sessions using their standard webcams and microphones. Software will extract: (1) vocal features (e.g., jitter, MFCCs) from audio; (2) mean nasal tip temperature and variability from video frames processed with a pre-trained CNN (e.g., OpenFace) and pseudo-thermal algorithms estimating blood flow; and (3) pupillary diameter and constriction velocity via webcam-based eye tracking. A Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture, building on Picard's prior work, will model these time-series features against Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) reports of stress and PHQ-8 scores, accounting for within-person random effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, validated framework for remote, multi-physiological signal fusion without specialized hardware, advancing the field of affective computing towards more accessible and comprehensive digital phenotyping. It will yield new insights into the temporal coordination of autonomic (thermal, pupillary) and vocal markers of distress, contributing to personalized prediction models for mental health. The study design and open-source tools will also inform the responsible neuroethics of remote sensing.
proposals.novelty: While remote vocal analysis exists, the integration of webcam-derived pseudo-thermal imaging and pupillometry as complementary, non-contact stress signals within a personalized MERF model for depression assessment is novel. This moves beyond lab-based, single-modality studies to a practical, multi-modal approach suitable for real-world deployment.

proposals.title: Long-Range Remote Photoplethysmography for Unobtrusive Assessment of Affective and Pain States in Naturalistic Settings
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in affective computing and digital health, led by Picard, demonstrates the value of physiological signals like heart rate variability for assessing pain, anxiety, and depression. However, existing camera-based rPPG methods are limited to short ranges (<3m), constraining their use to clinical or lab settings. Reliable long-range physiological sensing is needed to study real-world experiences, vocalizations, and social interactions in classrooms, homes, and communities without intrusive wearables.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a camera system combining novel hardware (e.g., high-sensitivity sensors, optimized optical filters) and a tailored software pipeline can reliably extract physiological pulsatility signals (rPPG) from facial regions at distances beyond 3 meters, and that these signals will correlate with self-reported and behavioral markers of affective states and pain.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype using a scientific CMOS camera with enhanced near-infrared sensitivity and a bandpass filter (520-590 nm) to optimize for blood volume pulse contrast. We will collect synchronized video and ground-truth ECG data from participants during controlled stressor tasks and resting periods at distances of 1m, 3m, and 5m. The software pipeline will use the POS algorithm for robust rPPG extraction, followed by a Mixed Effects Random Forest model to personalize pulse wave analysis and predict affective state severity, accounting for individual baselines and motion artifacts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated hardware-software framework for long-range, contactless physiology measurement, directly enabling new studies of mental health and pain in naturalistic group settings. It contributes to building responsible neurotech by advancing less intrusive monitoring methods and addresses a key technical bottleneck for scaling collaborative online health programs and ecological momentary assessment.
proposals.novelty: While rPPG is established, the systematic co-design of optical hardware and personalized machine learning models for distances >3m in real-world environments is novel. This moves beyond lab demonstrations to address the practical signal-quality challenges of distance, lighting variance, and partial occlusion faced in community and educational deployments.

proposals.title: Transfer Learning from Contact ECG to Non-Contact Audio for Multimodal Assessment of Pain and Depression
proposals.motivation: Heart rate variability (HRV) derived from electrocardiograms (ECG) is a well-established, non-invasive biomarker for autonomic nervous system dysregulation, relevant to affective states like pain, anxiety, and depression. However, ECG requires obtrusive contact sensors, limiting ecological monitoring. Non-contact vocal and audio analysis offers a promising alternative but lacks robust, physiologically-grounded feature sets for mental health assessment. Bridging this gap is critical for developing scalable, passive sensing tools for longitudinal health monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: Feature representations and classification models trained on gold-standard ECG-derived HRV can be effectively transferred to non-contact audio signals containing vocalizations and cardiac-synchronous sounds, enabling the prediction of self-reported pain and depression severity with comparable fidelity to contact-based methods.
proposals.method: We will collect a multimodal dataset from student participants during controlled stress and rest tasks, synchronizing high-fidelity ECG with audio recordings. Established time-domain, frequency-domain, and non-linear HRV features (e.g., RMSSD, LF/HF ratio, SD1/SD2) will be extracted from the ECG ground truth. Using a mixed-effects random forest (MERF) framework, we will first build a personalized model predicting depression/pain scores from ECG-HRV. We will then use transfer learning: the pre-trained MERF's learned feature representations will be used to guide the extraction and selection of analogous acoustic features (e.g., from phonation, pitch variability, and ballistocardiographic signals in audio) for a secondary model, evaluating its predictive performance against the ECG benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, validated mapping between contact-based physiological biomarkers and non-contact audio correlates, advancing the field of affective computing. It will deliver a new computational method for passive, privacy-sensitive assessment of pain and depressive states, directly contributing to the development of responsible neurotech for community and clinical health monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While HRV analysis and audio-based affect recognition are established separately, this proposal innovates by directly transferring the robust statistical structure of ECG-based HRV models to the audio domain, creating a physiologically-informed acoustic analysis pipeline rather than relying solely on generic audio features.

proposals.title: Multimodal Fusion of Remote Physiology, Facial Action Units, and Vocal Prosody for Continuous, Personalized Stress Assessment in Daily Life
proposals.motivation: Chronic stress is a significant precursor to clinical depression and anxiety, yet current assessment methods are often intrusive, episodic, and lack ecological validity. Pre-2026 affective computing research has shown promise in using individual modalities like heart rate variability (HRV) from wearables, facial expressions, or voice for stress detection, but each has limitations in real-world scenarios due to noise and context. A robust, continuous, and remote assessment tool is critically needed for early intervention and personalized mental health monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal fusion model combining remote physiology (e.g., electrodermal activity, HRV), facial action units (AUs), and vocal prosody features will significantly outperform unimodal or bimodal approaches in accurately and reliably classifying acute stress states during naturalistic, daily activities.
proposals.method: We will collect a novel dataset from student participants during controlled stress tasks (e.g., Trier Social Stress Test) and longitudinal free-living periods using an Empatica E4 wristband for physiology, a smartphone front camera for facial video, and phone audio for vocalizations. Features will include time-domain HRV, skin conductance level, a subset of AUs (e.g., AU4 brow lowerer, AU12 lip corner puller) from OpenFace, and prosodic features (pitch, jitter, intensity) from openSMILE. We will employ a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture for model fusion, which can account for both population-level patterns and personalized, subject-specific random effects to improve generalizability and personalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, ecologically valid multimodal dataset for stress research, 2) a validated MERF-based fusion framework for personalized affective computing that handles missing data common in real-world settings, and 3) empirical evidence on the relative and combined informativeness of physiology, face, and voice for remote stress assessment, advancing towards practical digital phenotyping tools.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has fused two modalities (e.g., face and voice), the integration of continuous, wearable physiology with computer vision and audio analysis for stress, using a MERF model to personalize predictions, is a novel synthesis of Picard's group expertise in personalized machine learning and multimodal affective sensing.

proposals.title: Personalized Stress Assessment via End-to-End Deep Learning on Raw Photoplethysmography Time-Series
proposals.motivation: Chronic stress is a significant precursor to clinical anxiety and depression, yet its objective, continuous measurement remains a challenge. Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) from consumer wearables offers a scalable, non-invasive signal rich with physiological markers of autonomic arousal linked to stress. However, existing methods often rely on handcrafted heart-rate-variability features, which can be noisy and fail to capture complex temporal dynamics in raw signals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that end-to-end time-series deep learning models, applied directly to raw rPPG waveforms, can more accurately and personally classify acute stress states than models using engineered features, by learning subtle, individualized physiological patterns.
proposals.method: We will collect raw rPPG data from wrist-worn devices during controlled lab stressors (e.g., Trier Social Stress Test) and naturalistic student assessments. We will develop and compare two deep learning architectures: a bidirectional LSTM with attention mechanisms for temporal modeling and a lightweight Transformer encoder adapted for long physiological sequences. Models will be trained for binary stress classification, with personalization achieved via mixed-effects random forest layers or fine-tuning on individual baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational method for passive, personalized stress sensing using ubiquitous wearable data. It will advance affective computing by demonstrating the value of raw signal modeling over feature engineering and provide a tool for longitudinal studies on the relationship between stress, time, and the development of mood disorders.
proposals.novelty: While deep learning has been applied to ECG, its direct application to noisier, remote PPG signals for stress classification is nascent. The novelty lies in the end-to-end processing of raw rPPG and the integration of personalization techniques from clinical depression modeling into a time-series stress framework.

proposals.title: Remote Multimodal Sensing of Cognitive Flow: A Mixed-Effects Forest Approach for Personalized Positive State Detection
proposals.motivation: While significant research, including Picard's own work, has focused on detecting negative states like depression and anxiety from remote physiology, the automated detection of positive cognitive states like flow and deep engagement remains underexplored. This is a critical gap for applications in personalized learning, workplace wellness, and mental health, where understanding and fostering optimal experience is as important as mitigating distress. Pre-2026 advances in wearable sensors and machine learning for affective computing create a timely opportunity to address this dual problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combination of vocal prosody, electrodermal activity, and heart rate variability, analyzed via a personalized machine learning model, can reliably distinguish states of cognitive flow from states of distraction or low engagement in naturalistic settings.
proposals.method: We will collect multimodal data (audio, EDA, PPG) from students during focused study sessions and self-reported periods of distraction, using wearable devices and smartphone microphones. Building on Picard's recent methodological work, we will employ a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) algorithm to model both population-level patterns and individual-specific deviations in physiological signatures. Features will include vocal jitter/shimmer, speech rate, EDA tonic/phasic components, and HRV metrics (RMSSD, LF/HF ratio). The model will be trained to predict self-reported and task-performance-based flow labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, personalized model for detecting a positive cognitive state from passive, remote sensing, expanding the scope of affective computing beyond negative affect. It will provide a methodological framework for the 'dual problem' of state detection, enabling future systems that not only alleviate suffering but also promote flourishing. The findings will directly inform the design of more responsive educational and digital health technologies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the personalized MERF framework, recently validated for depression, to the distinct challenge of identifying flow—a positive, high-cognitive-load state—and in explicitly framing this as the complementary objective to distress detection within a responsible neurotech paradigm.

proposals.title: Personalized Stress Detection from Vocal Prosody and Facial Action Units Using a Single Webcam
proposals.motivation: Current ambulatory stress detection often relies on multiple wired physiological sensors (e.g., ECG, EDA), which are cumbersome, socially obtrusive, and impractical for long-term, real-world monitoring. This limits ecological validity and adherence, particularly in vulnerable populations like students with anxiety or depression. A shift to a single, non-contact sensor is crucial for scalable, practical mental health monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal machine learning model, fusing vocal prosody features and facial action unit (AU) dynamics captured via a standard webcam, can detect acute stress episodes with accuracy comparable to multi-sensor physiological baselines, while personalization via mixed-effects modeling will significantly improve performance.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized webcam audio/video and multi-sensor physiological (EDA, ECG) data from students during controlled stress-induction tasks (e.g., Trier Social Stress Test) and naturalistic study sessions. From audio, we will extract prosodic features (pitch, jitter, intensity, speaking rate). From video, we will extract AU intensities (e.g., AU4, AU7, AU12) using OpenFace. A Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture will be employed, where fixed effects are the multimodal features and random effects account for individual subject baselines, enabling personalized stress prediction from the single-sensor input.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, practical framework for minimally invasive stress assessment, directly advancing the goal of ecological mental health monitoring. It will contribute a novel, personalized MERF model for multimodal behavioral signal fusion and a public dataset of synchronized webcam and physiological stress responses.
proposals.novelty: While vocal or facial stress analysis exists, the personalized fusion of both modalities within a MERF framework, using only a webcam to replace a sensor suite, is novel. This approach directly addresses the socio-technical challenge of obtrusiveness raised in neuroethics, moving towards responsible, practical neurotech.

proposals.title: Causal Inference from Wearable Sensor Data: Testing if Behavioral Changes Drive Self-Reported Mood in Depression and Anxiety
proposals.motivation: Wearable sensors show strong correlations between behaviors (e.g., vocal prosody, activity, sleep) and self-reported mental states like depression and anxiety. However, the dominant paradigm treats sensor data as a passive biomarker, leaving the causal direction—whether changes in behavior can *cause* changes in mental state—untested. Establishing causality is critical for designing effective, sensor-informed behavioral interventions that actively improve mental health, moving beyond passive monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, sensor-measured behavioral changes (e.g., increased vocal energy, more regular circadian movement) causally precede and drive improvements in subsequent self-reported depression and anxiety severity, rather than merely reflecting them.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal, multi-modal data (audio, accelerometer, phone use) from student cohorts, collected during studies on depression and anxiety. Using causal inference methods for observational data, such as Structural Nested Mean Models (SNMMs) and targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE), we will test for causal effects of lagged behavioral features on future mood reports, controlling for confounders. This will be complemented by a micro-intervention study where participants are randomly prompted to enact specific sensor-observable behaviors (e.g., social vocalization, brisk walking) to test for immediate causal effects on state anxiety.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for causal analysis in digital phenotyping, shifting the field from correlation to causation. It will identify which sensor-derived behaviors are most promising as active intervention targets, directly informing the development of personalized, just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) for mental health.
proposals.novelty: While machine learning models (e.g., Random Forests) excel at prediction, applying formal causal inference models to high-frequency sensor data to test reverse causality in mental health is novel. It challenges the assumption that sensor data is merely a reflective output of internal state.

proposals.title: Opportunistic Sensing for Ecological Momentary Assessment: Validating Sparse Vocal and Physiological Samples for Depression and Anxiety Prediction
proposals.motivation: Continuous wearable sensing for mental health monitoring creates significant user burden, primarily due to rapid battery drain, which reduces compliance and long-term feasibility. This is a critical barrier to translating research tools like those for predicting depression severity into real-world, always-available support systems. A method that maintains predictive accuracy while drastically reducing power consumption is essential for sustainable, equitable digital health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sparse, opportunistic samples of vocal acoustics and physiological data (e.g., heart rate), collected only during natural device interactions (e.g., phone calls, screen unlocks), can achieve predictive accuracy for depression and anxiety severity comparable to models trained on continuous, high-frequency sensor streams.
proposals.method: We will deploy a modified version of our existing smartphone sensing platform to a cohort of university students, collecting both continuous sensor data and timestamped logs of opportunistic sampling triggers. We will extract established vocal prosody features (e.g., jitter, shimmer, MFCCs) and heart rate variability from these sparse samples. Using a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture—which models both population-level and individual-specific effects—we will train models on the opportunistic data to predict PHQ-9 and GAD-7 scores, benchmarking them against models trained on the continuous data baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a methodological framework for ultra-low-power mental health sensing that prioritizes user sustainability. It will yield concrete evidence on the minimal sampling rates required for reliable prediction, directly informing the design of next-generation clinical tools and responsible neurotech. The findings will advance the field towards practical, long-term monitoring solutions that respect user autonomy and device constraints.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses continuous or scheduled sampling, this proposal explicitly treats device interaction events as a valid, information-rich sampling stratum for mental health prediction. It combines the MERF's strength in personalized modeling with a novel, resource-conscious data acquisition strategy grounded in real-world human-computer interaction patterns.

proposals.title: Personalized Physiology: A Mixed-Effects Forest Approach to Quantifying Inter-Individual Variability in Pain and Depression Markers
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing and digital health research often seeks universal physiological markers (e.g., from voice, EDA, HRV) for states like pain and depression. However, pre-2026 work by Picard and others suggests high inter-individual variability in physiological responses. Relying on generalized models risks poor performance and misdiagnosis in real-world applications, undermining personalized health interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: Objective physiological markers for affective states are not universal but are highly personalized; models that explicitly account for individual random effects will significantly outperform pooled, one-size-fits-all models in predicting pain experience and depression severity.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing multi-modal datasets (e.g., BioVid, DAIC-WOZ) containing physiological signals (ECG, EDA, vocal acoustics) and self-reported pain/depression labels. We will employ Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF), a pre-2026 algorithm aligning with Picard's recent work, to model both population-level fixed effects and subject-specific random effects. Model performance (MERF vs. standard RF/pooled models) will be rigorously compared using personalized leave-one-subject-out cross-validation, quantifying the variance explained by individual random effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide quantitative evidence for the degree of personalization needed in affective sensing, moving the field beyond universal biomarkers. It will deliver a robust, interpretable modeling framework (MERF) for building personalized digital health diagnostics, directly informing the development of more ethical and effective neurotechnologies by highlighting individual differences.
proposals.novelty: While personalization is acknowledged, this study systematically quantifies the *failure* of universal markers using state-of-the-art mixed-effects machine learning, challenging a core assumption in much automated affect recognition research. It uniquely bridges Picard's work on personalized depression prediction with the neuroethics of responsible, individualized biomarker development.

proposals.title: Generalizing Affective Computing Markers: Validating Physiological and Vocal Predictors of Depression and Anxiety in Older Adult and Clinical Populations
proposals.motivation: Prior work, including from our group, has successfully identified digital biomarkers for depression and anxiety using wearable sensors and vocal analysis, but validation has been largely confined to young, healthy student populations. This limits clinical translation, as the expression and correlates of affective states may differ significantly in older adults and clinical cohorts due to age, comorbidities, and medication effects. Establishing the generalizability of these markers is a critical, unmet step toward equitable and effective personalized mental health monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that physiological (e.g., electrodermal activity, heart rate variability) and vocal prosody markers previously predictive of depression/anxiety severity in students will retain predictive validity in older adult and outpatient clinical populations, but that their relative importance and optimal multimodal fusion models will differ across groups.
proposals.method: We will recruit three cohorts: a replication student cohort (N=100), an older adult cohort (N=80, age 65+), and a clinical cohort (N=80, diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder). All participants will wear an Empatica E4 wristband for one week and complete brief daily vocal diary tasks via smartphone, alongside ecological momentary assessment of mood and anxiety. We will extract established feature sets (time/frequency domain physiology, openSMILE vocal features). Using Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF)—a method we previously developed to handle repeated measures—we will build personalized prediction models for depression (PHQ-9) and anxiety (GAD-7) scores, comparing feature importance and model performance across cohorts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct test of the generalizability of digital affective biomarkers beyond university settings. It will yield cohort-specific model insights crucial for tailoring future interventions and establish a methodological framework for responsible, population-specific model development in affective computing. The findings will directly inform the socio-technical challenges of deploying neurotech in diverse communities.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies validate markers within a single population, this is a deliberate, head-to-head validation and comparison of an established multimodal sensing pipeline and advanced personalization algorithm (MERF) across demographically and clinically distinct groups, explicitly testing for transferability rather than assuming it.

proposals.title: A Just-in-Time Adaptive Intervention (JITAI) System for Stress Management Using Real-Time Vocal and Physiological Markers
proposals.motivation: Chronic stress is a significant precursor to clinical depression and anxiety, yet existing interventions are often reactive and poorly timed. Pre-2026 research by Picard and others has identified promising multimodal markers of stress, including vocal prosody and autonomic nervous system activity, but these have not been systematically integrated into closed-loop, real-time support systems. There is a critical need for scalable, personalized, and preventative tools that can intervene at moments of rising stress before it escalates.
proposals.hypothesis: A JITAI system that uses a personalized machine learning model to detect acute stress from real-time vocal and wearable sensor data can trigger effective, context-aware micro-interventions, leading to a measurable reduction in self-reported stress and physiological arousal compared to a randomized control condition.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone and wearable-based system (using E4 wristbands) to collect continuous audio (processed on-device for privacy) and physiological data (EDA, HRV). A personalized Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) model, building on Picard's prior work, will be trained on individual baseline data to classify stress states. Upon detection of a high-probability stress event, the system will trigger a randomly assigned micro-intervention (e.g., guided breathing, cognitive reframing prompt) from a validated library. The study will employ an N-of-1 randomized controlled trial design with student participants over a 4-week period.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, empirically tested JITAI architecture for mental health. It will provide new evidence on the efficacy of real-time, multimodal stress intervention and advance methods for personalizing MERF models in dynamic, everyday contexts. The project will also generate a framework for responsible neurotech deployment in real-time affective computing.
proposals.novelty: While JITAIs exist, this proposal uniquely integrates Picard's work on personalized MERF models for depression with real-time vocal stress markers to drive the intervention loop. The focus on a closed-loop system that uses inherently private, on-device audio processing for timely intervention in naturalistic settings is a distinct technical and ethical advance.

proposals.title: Personalized, Low-Burden Ecological Momentary Assessment: A Hybrid Passive Sensing and Vocal Biomarker Approach for Predicting Pain and Depression Fluctuations
proposals.motivation: Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) is crucial for capturing dynamic experiences like pain and depression, but high participant burden from frequent e-diary prompts leads to dropout and poor data quality, limiting clinical utility. Pre-2026, passive sensing from smartphones and wearables offers rich behavioral data, but methods to reliably infer subjective states without intrusive self-report remain underdeveloped, especially for vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid EMA model that intelligently triggers brief, voice-based self-report prompts based on passive sensor data (e.g., mobility, phone use, vocal prosody from ambient speech) will reduce participant burden and improve prediction of pain and depression severity compared to fixed-interval e-diaries.
proposals.method: We will develop a smartphone app using a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture to personalize the timing of prompts. The model will continuously analyze passive streams (GPS, accelerometer, call logs, app usage) and short audio snippets (processed on-device for prosodic features like pitch and energy) to estimate 'risk' of a clinically meaningful shift in state. Prompts for ultra-brief (≤30 sec) vocal or single-item diary entries will be triggered adaptively when uncertainty is high, minimizing unnecessary interruptions. We will validate this in a 4-week study with chronic pain and/or depressed students, comparing dropout rates and prediction accuracy against a control group with random-interval prompts.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, ethically-considered framework for adaptive EMA that balances data richness with participant autonomy, directly addressing dropout. It will advance methods for personalizing mental and physical health monitoring by demonstrating how vocal biomarkers and passive sensing can be fused to reduce self-report burden while maintaining predictive validity for clinical constructs.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its use of on-device vocal prosody analysis from ambient speech as a passive sensor to guide adaptive EMA triggering, combined with a MERF model that personalizes not just predictions but the very timing of assessment, moving beyond static schedule optimization.

proposals.title: Forecasting Psychophysiological Stress: Adapting Financial Time-Series Models for Wearable Sensor Data
proposals.motivation: Wearable sensors provide rich, continuous physiological data (e.g., EDA, HRV, vocal prosody) relevant to stress, anxiety, and depression, but current models often focus on retrospective classification rather than proactive forecasting. The ability to predict future stress states would enable timely, personalized interventions, a core goal of affective computing and digital mental health. Financial markets have pioneered sophisticated models for forecasting volatile, noisy time-series, presenting a promising but underexplored resource for psychophysiology.
proposals.hypothesis: Time-series forecasting architectures developed for financial data (e.g., volatility modeling, regime-switching) can be effectively adapted and personalized to forecast near-future stress severity from multimodal wearable sensor streams, outperforming standard sequential models.
proposals.method: We will adapt models like Temporal Fusion Transformers (TFTs) and GARCH-family models, which handle volatility and exogenous variables in finance, to forecast stress levels (self-reported and derived from features like vocal tension and electrodermal activity). Using existing longitudinal student datasets (e.g., from studies on anxiety and depression), we will treat physiological streams as multivariate financial-like series. We will employ mixed-effects random forests for personalization, integrating them into the forecasting pipeline to account for individual baselines, akin to modeling individual 'market' behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel forecasting framework for affective states, moving beyond detection to prediction. It will contribute a validated method for translating financial econometrics to personal health analytics. The project also advances the neuroethics of predictive neurotech by examining the socio-technical challenges of forecasting internal states.
proposals.novelty: While time-series models are used in health, the direct conceptual and architectural translation of volatility-focused financial forecasting models to psychophysiological stress prediction is novel. The integration of personalization techniques (mixed-effects forests) within these adapted architectures for affective forecasting is also unexplored.

proposals.title: The Minimal Viable Sensor: Identifying a Single, High-Fidelity Vocal Biomarker for Population-Level Depression Screening
proposals.motivation: Scalable, low-cost screening for mental health conditions like depression is a critical public health need, but existing multi-sensor approaches are often too complex and expensive for widespread deployment. Pre-2026 research, including Picard's work on affective computing and vocal biomarkers, suggests that voice may be a rich, passive, and privacy-preserving signal of affective state. Determining if a single modality can achieve sufficient predictive validity is essential for developing equitable, accessible screening tools.
proposals.hypothesis: That short-duration, naturalistic speech samples, analyzed for prosodic, spectral, and temporal features, will provide predictive power for depression severity comparable to multi-modal models, making it the most viable single sensor for large-scale screening.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing multi-modal datasets (e.g., with audio, EDA, accelerometry) from prior studies of depression and anxiety in student populations. Using a nested feature selection and model comparison framework, we will train Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF) and regularized linear models (LASSO) on vocal features alone versus all other sensor modalities. Model performance will be evaluated via cross-validated prediction of depression severity scores (e.g., PHQ-8), with a focus on generalizability across demographic subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for a cost-effective, single-sensor screening paradigm, directly informing the design of future public health tools. It will also contribute a methodological framework for rigorously comparing sensor modalities and advance the neuroethical discourse on responsible neurotech by prioritizing minimal, interpretable data collection.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal sensing is common, systematically identifying a *minimal viable sensor* with rigorous comparison to multi-modal baselines for depression screening is novel. The approach uniquely combines Picard's expertise in MERF for personalized prediction with a principled feature reduction strategy to bridge the gap between research-grade sensing and public health utility.

proposals.title: Inverse Behavioral Modeling: Predicting Smartphone-Mediated Activity Patterns from High-Stress States
proposals.motivation: While much affective computing research focuses on detecting stress from behavior, the inverse problem—predicting likely behavioral adaptations *given* a known high-stress state—remains underexplored. This is crucial for developing anticipatory, personalized digital health interventions that can nudge individuals towards healthier coping behaviors before maladaptive patterns solidify, particularly in populations like students vulnerable to anxiety and depression.
proposals.hypothesis: Given a known high-stress state, an individual's subsequent smartphone usage and activity patterns are not random but follow a predictable, personalized distribution that can be modeled, with deviations from this predicted pattern indicating either recovery or escalation of distress.
proposals.method: We will collect multimodal sensor data (GPS, app usage, screen time, accelerometry) and self-reported stress from student participants over 8 weeks. Using a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture—which Picard's group has successfully applied to depression prediction—we will model the conditional probability distribution of behavioral features (e.g., location entropy, social app duration) given a high-stress label. The model will incorporate personal baselines (random effects) and population-level stress-behavior mappings (fixed effects). We will validate predictions against held-out behavioral ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for modeling stress-contingent behavior, moving beyond detection to prediction. It will contribute a new method for personalizing just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) in mental health by anticipating behavioral needs. The findings will also offer insights into the behavioral phenotype of stress, informing neuroethical discussions on predictive neurotech.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard causal modeling direction in affective computing, framing stress as the input and behavior as the output. The application of MERFs to this inverse problem, to capture both group-level trends and personal idiosyncrasies in stress-response behaviors, is a novel methodological advance.

proposals.title: Predicting and Preempting Dropout in Ecological Momentary Assessment Studies of Depression and Anxiety Using Wearable Sensor Data and Mixed Effects Random Forests
proposals.motivation: Longitudinal studies using ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to track mental health constructs like depression and anxiety are vital for understanding real-world experience, but high participant dropout and non-compliance threaten data validity and statistical power. Pre-2026, passive sensor data from wearables has shown promise for predicting clinical states, but its application to predicting and preempting study-specific behaviors like non-response remains underexplored. This directly impacts the feasibility and quality of research in affective computing and digital phenotyping.
proposals.hypothesis: That a model combining passive wearable sensor data (e.g., vocal prosody, activity, sleep) with baseline participant characteristics can reliably predict impending EMA non-compliance, and that timely, personalized digital interventions based on these predictions can significantly improve participant adherence.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 6-week EMA study with 150 students, collecting daily self-reports on mood and anxiety via smartphone, alongside continuous data from wrist-worn sensors (e.g., Empatica E4) capturing movement, electrodermal activity, and vocal snippets from phone interactions. We will employ Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF), a method previously used by the researcher for depression severity, to build personalized prediction models of missed prompts, using sensor-derived features (e.g., reduced vocal energy, social isolation metrics, sleep disruption) as time-varying inputs. A second phase will test a preemptive intervention: participants flagged as high-risk for non-compliance will receive tailored, supportive notifications co-designed with participants from prior COIL (Collaborative Online International Learning) programs to foster engagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for adaptive study design that actively mitigates attrition bias. It will contribute to the responsible neurotech and health communities by demonstrating an ethical, participant-centric use of sensor data to improve research integrity. The resulting models and intervention strategies will be a step toward more sustainable and reliable longitudinal mental health monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While sensor data predicts clinical states, its use to forecast and actively improve *study compliance* itself is a novel application. The approach uniquely integrates the personalization of MERF with a preemptive, community-informed intervention strategy, moving beyond prediction to action within the research paradigm.

proposals.title: Personalized Mixed-Effects Random Forests for Adaptive Detection of Clinical Pain and Depression from Ambulatory ACC and EDA
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing models for pain and depression often assume population-level physiological signatures, leading to poor generalization for individuals whose autonomic (EDA) and movement (ACC) patterns deviate from the norm. This one-size-fits-all approach limits the clinical utility of wearable-based monitoring, particularly for chronic conditions where personal baselines and reactivity are highly variable. Developing truly personalized models is a critical step toward reliable, real-world digital phenotyping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a mixed-effects modeling framework, which explicitly separates population-level (fixed) effects from individual-level (random) effects, will significantly outperform generic models in detecting and predicting the severity of pain and depressive episodes from longitudinal wearable sensor data.
proposals.method: We will collect multi-week ambulatory ACC and EDA data from patients with comorbid chronic pain and depression, alongside ecological momentary assessments of pain and mood. We will adapt the Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) algorithm, previously applied to depression prediction, to model EDA tonic/phasic components and ACC-derived activity/rest cycles. The model will learn shared feature importance (fixed effects) while simultaneously adapting its tree structures to individual-specific physiological baselines and reactivity patterns (random effects).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, personalized framework for detecting pain and depression flares from wearable sensors, moving beyond population averages. It will contribute a novel methodological application of MERF to multimodal physiological time-series data and provide new insights into the individual temporal dynamics linking autonomic arousal, movement, and subjective experience in comorbid conditions.
proposals.novelty: While MERFs have been used for depression, their application to model *individual-specific* ACC/EDA signatures for *both* pain and depression is novel. The core innovation is the explicit modeling of personal physiological 'footprints' as random effects within a powerful, non-parametric ensemble learner, enabling adaptation without requiring vast per-patient data.
