proposals.title: Multimodal Fusion of Vocal, Cardiac, and Thermal Signals for Personalized and Context-Aware Detection of Pain and Affective Distress
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing systems for monitoring conditions like pain and depression often rely on single modalities (e.g., vocal acoustics or actigraphy), leading to high false alarm rates due to contextual confounds like physical exertion or ambient stress. Pre-2026 work by Picard and others highlights the need for more robust, personalized models that can disambiguate the physiological signatures of clinical distress from other states. Integrating complementary, wearable-friendly sensors can provide a richer, context-aware signal of autonomic nervous system activity, which is central to the experience of pain and affective disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multimodal sensor suite combining vocal features, photoplethysmography (PPG)-derived heart rate variability (HRV), and continuous skin temperature, when analyzed with a personalized temporal modeling framework, will significantly improve the accuracy and specificity of detecting pain episodes and depressive symptom severity compared to unimodal approaches.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized data from a wearable (Empatica E4) providing PPG and temperature, and smartphone audio recordings during structured lab inductions and longitudinal field studies with chronic pain and student populations. Features will include vocal prosody and spectral measures, HRV indices (RMSSD, LF/HF), and skin temperature trends. We will employ Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF), a method successfully used in Picard's prior depression work, to model both population-level and participant-specific effects from the multimodal time-series data, explicitly modeling temporal dependencies to reduce false alarms.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, multimodal framework for personalized affective state assessment that moves beyond lab settings. It will provide new evidence on the synergistic value of vocal, cardiac, and thermal signals for disambiguating distress. The resulting open-source tools and findings will advance the development of more reliable digital phenotyping for mental and physical health monitoring.
proposals.novelty: While multimodal sensing exists, the novel integration lies in using MERF for personalized temporal fusion of these specific modalities to directly address the false alarm problem in real-world detection of pain and comorbid depression, tightly aligning with Picard's focus on ethical, responsible, and clinically actionable neurotechnology.

proposals.title: Longitudinal, Real-World Validation of a Mixed-Effects Random Forest for Depression Severity Detection from Vocal and Temporal Biomarkers in Home Environments
proposals.motivation: Current algorithms for detecting depression and pain states are often validated in controlled lab settings or short-term studies, limiting their ecological validity and generalizability to daily life. For affective computing to transform mental health monitoring, it must prove reliable in the complex, longitudinal context of patients' homes, where factors like time, daily routines, and natural vocalizations critically shape experience.
proposals.hypothesis: A mixed-effects random forest model, previously developed for personalized depression prediction, will maintain clinically significant accuracy and robustness when validated over extended periods (6+ months) in patients' natural home environments, with its performance being influenced by longitudinal vocal patterns and temporal covariates.
proposals.method: We will deploy a passive, smartphone-based sensing platform to collect continuous audio snippets (processed for prosodic and spectral features) and self-reported ecological momentary assessments (EMA) of mood and pain in a large cohort (N>200) with major depressive disorder. The core detection algorithm will be the previously established Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF), which models both fixed effects (e.g., vocal features, time of day) and random participant-specific effects. We will conduct a 12-month study, using the first 6 months for personalization and the latter 6 for prospective validation against monthly clinician-administered PHQ-9 scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide one of the first large-scale evidence bases for the long-term, real-world validity of a personalized machine learning model for mental health monitoring. It will yield critical insights into the stability of vocal biomarkers over time and establish a methodological framework for responsible, ecologically valid neurotech validation outside the clinic.
proposals.novelty: While MERFs have shown promise for personalization, their deployment and rigorous validation over extended periods in completely unstructured home settings is novel. This study uniquely integrates Picard's focus on temporal dynamics, natural vocalizations, and responsible neurotech into a longitudinal validation paradigm.

proposals.title: Transfer Learning from Human Activity Recognition to Seizure Motion Pattern Identification: A Personalized, Wearable-Sensor Approach
proposals.motivation: Automated detection of epileptic seizures using wearable sensors is crucial for patient safety and quality of life, but developing robust models is hindered by the scarcity of labeled seizure data. Pre-2026, large-scale, open-source datasets for human activity recognition (HAR) have enabled highly accurate models for classifying movements like walking, running, and falling. This project proposes to address the data scarcity in seizure detection by leveraging the rich, pre-trained feature representations from these HAR models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deep neural networks pre-trained on large-scale human activity recognition datasets will provide effective foundational feature extractors for wearable accelerometer and gyroscope data, enabling the accurate identification of motor seizure patterns with significantly less patient-specific labeled data than training from scratch.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune pre-trained convolutional neural networks (CNNs) or transformer architectures (e.g., models from the HAR benchmark repository) on multi-modal wearable sensor data (accelerometer, gyroscope) collected from epilepsy patients. The base model layers will be frozen initially, and only the final classification layers will be trained on a smaller dataset of annotated seizure motions. We will employ mixed-effects random forest techniques, aligning with Picard's recent work, to personalize predictions by modeling both population-level seizure patterns and individual patient deviations.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-efficient pipeline for personalized seizure detection that reduces the burden of data collection. It will advance the methodological integration of transfer learning and personalization techniques in digital health. Furthermore, it will provide empirical evidence for the shared kinematic representations between general human activities and pathological motor patterns.
proposals.novelty: While transfer learning is common in computer vision, its application from general HAR to the specific, critical domain of seizure motion analysis is novel. The combination of deep transfer learning with mixed-effects models for personalization in this clinical context is a non-obvious and promising synthesis of pre-2026 state-of-the-art methods.

proposals.title: Inverse Modeling of Autonomic Seizure Physiology from Wearable ACC and EDA Data
proposals.motivation: Epileptic seizures profoundly disrupt autonomic nervous system (ANS) function, manifesting in measurable changes in movement (via accelerometry/ACC) and electrodermal activity (EDA). Pre-2026 work has largely focused on using these signals for seizure detection. However, the inverse problem—using these peripheral data streams to infer and simulate the underlying, personalized ANS state dynamics during a seizure—remains unexplored. This limits our ability to model the subjective, physiological experience of a seizure and to develop personalized neurostimulation or biofeedback interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that time-series data from wrist-worn ACC and EDA sensors during seizures contain sufficient information to inversely estimate and computationally simulate key parameters of a patient-specific, coupled ANS model, capturing the distinctive physiological trajectory of ictal and post-ictal states.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized ACC and EDA data from the Empatica E4 wristband during seizures in a monitored epilepsy monitoring unit. The core technical approach involves formulating an inverse problem where a computational ANS model (e.g., a system of differential equations governing sympathetic/parasympathetic tone and sudomotor activity) is fitted to the observed data. We will employ a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) framework, extending Picard's prior work, to personalize model parameters from population priors while capturing individual variability. Bayesian optimization will be used to solve the inverse problem and estimate the latent ANS state sequence that best explains the observed ACC (motor agitation/tonicity) and EDA (sympathetic arousal) signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel computational methodology for inferring latent neurophysiological states from wearable sensor data, moving beyond detection to simulation; 2) personalized digital phenotypes of ANS dysregulation during seizures, which could correlate with subjective experiences of pain or anxiety; and 3) a foundational model for future closed-loop systems that modulate ANS state, contributing to responsible neurotech development.
proposals.novelty: While seizure detection from wearables is established, the inverse modeling approach to simulate the underlying ANS physiology is novel. The application of MERFs, typically used for clinical prediction, to parameterize and personalize a dynamical systems model for ANS simulation represents a significant methodological innovation.

proposals.title: A Closed-Loop Biofeedback System for Stress-Induced Seizure Prophylaxis: A Pilot Study in Epilepsy
proposals.motivation: Stress is a well-documented, pre-2026 trigger for epileptic seizures, yet current neurotechnology primarily focuses on detection and responsive stimulation after seizure onset. This reactive paradigm fails to address the prodromal stress state that may precipitate the event. A proactive, preventative approach that promotes autonomic calm could improve quality of life and reduce seizure burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a wearable device using multimodal physiological sensors can detect prodromal stress signatures in individuals with epilepsy and that delivering real-time, personalized biofeedback to induce a calm state can reduce the likelihood of stress-triggered seizure events.
proposals.method: We will adapt a multi-sensor wearable (e.g., Empatica E4) to continuously measure electrodermal activity, heart rate variability, and skin temperature. A personalized Mixed Effects Random Forest model, trained on individual baseline and stress-labelled data, will classify stress states. Upon detection, the system will initiate a closed-loop auditory biofeedback protocol, modulating sound parameters (e.g., tone, rhythm) based on real-time physiological convergence toward a calmer state, creating a personalized neurovisceral feedback loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, proactive neurotechnology paradigm for epilepsy management, shifting from seizure detection to pre-seizure intervention. It will provide preliminary evidence on the feasibility of using biofeedback for seizure prophylaxis and yield new data on the temporal dynamics linking stress, autonomic regulation, and seizure susceptibility.
proposals.novelty: While biofeedback for general stress reduction exists, its integration into a closed-loop, wearable system explicitly designed to intercept the stress-to-seizure pathway in epilepsy is novel. The approach repurposes detection-oriented sensors for preventative intervention, creating a 'dual-use' therapeutic device.

proposals.title: Spectral Fusion for Affective Inference: A Multi-Modal Sensing Framework for In-Cabin Emotional Biometrics and Proactive Ambient Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Passenger well-being in shared and autonomous vehicles is a growing health and human-computer interaction challenge. Pre-2026 research in affective computing and wearable sensing shows that emotional states like anxiety and discomfort significantly impact experience and health outcomes, yet current in-cabin systems lack robust, passive, and nuanced sensing capabilities. This work addresses the need for non-invasive, continuous emotional assessment to enable truly responsive environments that support mental health and shared social experience.
proposals.hypothesis: Fusing visible, near-infrared, and thermal imaging data within a personalized machine learning framework will enable more accurate and robust inference of complex emotional states (e.g., anxiety, excitement) than any single modality, and that using these inferences to drive ambient adaptations will measurably improve subjective passenger well-being.
proposals.method: We will deploy a custom multi-spectral sensor array in a simulated cabin to collect synchronized visible (facial action units, pupillometry), NIR (subtle blood flow via photoplethysmography imaging), and thermal (localized temperature variations) data from participants during induced and naturalistic emotional episodes. Data will be processed using pre-2026 computer vision pipelines (OpenFace, custom rPPG) and fused using a Mixed Effects Random Forest architecture, extending Picard's prior work, to model both population-level spectral correlates of emotion and individual-specific variations. Inferred states will trigger scripted adaptations of lighting, climate, and audio for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel multi-spectral fusion dataset and benchmark for in-cabin emotional biometrics, 2) a personalized predictive model for clinical-grade affective states like anxiety and depression severity in a naturalistic setting, and 3) an initial framework for responsible, context-aware ambient adaptation that respects neuroethical principles of agency and privacy.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in applying spectral fusion not for object recognition, but for passive emotional phenotyping, and in coupling this biometric inference with a closed-loop ambient system designed using a neuroethics-by-design approach to enhance well-being proactively.

proposals.title: Ambient Auditory Regulation for Driver Affect: A Closed-Loop System Using Indirect Behavioral Signals and Real-Time Soundscape Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Driver stress and cognitive fatigue are significant, well-documented pre-2026 risk factors for road safety and long-term health, linked to anxiety and depression. Current in-vehicle affect-aware systems primarily focus on passive monitoring or explicit, often distracting, alerts. There is a critical need for non-invasive, implicit intervention strategies that support driver well-being and safety without adding cognitive load.
proposals.hypothesis: That a driver's cognitive-emotional state, inferred from indirect behavioral and vocal signals, can be effectively regulated in real-time by a dynamically synthesized, ambient auditory texture, leading to measurable reductions in self-reported stress and improvements in physiological markers of calmness.
proposals.method: We will collect a multimodal dataset of steering micro-movements, pedal dynamics, and cabin audio (speech, sighs) during simulated driving tasks designed to induce stress and fatigue. A Mixed Effects Random Forest model, extending Picard's prior work, will personalize the inference of affective state (stress/fatigue/calm). This state will parameterize a real-time granular synthesis engine, using pre-2026 methods like asynchronous granular synthesis or phase vocoding, to generate continuous, non-melodic sound textures (e.g., pulsing rhythms, filtered noise). A closed-loop controller will adapt texture parameters (e.g., tempo, spectral centroid) to counter inferred distress.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, closed-loop HCI paradigm for implicit affective regulation in vehicles, 2) a validated method for personalizing state inference from low-cost, indirect signals, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of synthesized auditory textures as a regulatory intervention, advancing the field beyond passive sensing.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of personalized behavioral affect inference with real-time, generative auditory synthesis for closed-loop regulation is novel, moving beyond descriptive analytics or pre-recorded sound playback to create an adaptive, ambient biofeedback system without wearables.

proposals.title: Affect-Preserving Video Compression for Long-Term In-Cabin Monitoring via Spatio-Temporal Auto-Regressive Models
proposals.motivation: Continuous in-cabin monitoring is crucial for studying affective dynamics like driver fatigue and frustration, and for enabling advanced safety systems. However, recording high-resolution video for extended periods creates prohibitive bandwidth and storage costs. Current compression standards (e.g., H.265) discard data uniformly, potentially degrading the subtle, temporally evolving cues (e.g., micro-expressions, posture shifts) most relevant to affective computing and mental health assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a model of long-term emotional dynamics with a Spatio-Temporal Auto-Regressive (STAR) texture model will enable a video compression framework that selectively preserves the visual data most predictive of affective state transitions, achieving high compression ratios without degrading performance on downstream emotion and behavior analysis tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stream compression framework. The first stream uses a pre-2026 STAR model to learn and compactly represent repetitive temporal textures (e.g., road scenery reflection, steady driving posture). The second stream employs a lightweight, recurrent neural network (e.g., an LSTM) trained on pre-2026 affective computing datasets to identify and prioritize facial/body regions and motion patterns predictive of emotional trajectories (e.g., growing fatigue). A rate-distortion optimization will then allocate bits, heavily compressing the STAR-modeled background while preserving high fidelity for the affectively salient spatio-temporal features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, application-aware video codec that makes long-duration, in-cabin affective studies technically and economically feasible. It contributes a new methodology for fusing physical signal models (STAR) with behavioral models (affective dynamics) for efficient sensing. The resulting compressed streams will directly serve downstream tools for analyzing temporal patterns in depression, anxiety, and adaptation during human-machine interaction.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior content-aware compression, this approach explicitly optimizes for preserving long-term *emotional dynamics* rather than short-term action recognition or generic saliency. It uniquely bridges the gap between low-level video texture modeling and high-level affective computing models for a constrained automotive application.

proposals.title: Inferring Driver Affective State from Indirect Vehicle and Audio Signals: A Study on Pain, Anxiety, and Depression Correlates
proposals.motivation: Direct physiological sensing (e.g., ECG, EDA) for emotion inference in real-world settings faces barriers related to obtrusiveness, cost, and long-term wearability, limiting scalability for mental health monitoring. The automotive cabin presents a rich, underutilized source of indirect behavioral signals. This research addresses the need for passive, scalable affective computing by leveraging data already available in modern vehicles, aligning with Picard's focus on real-world health applications like pain and depression.
proposals.hypothesis: Indirect, multi-modal signals from vehicle telemetry (steering, pedal input, speed variance) and in-cabin audio (vocalizations, sighs, silence patterns) contain sufficient information to infer clinically relevant affective states, including acute pain episodes and fluctuations in anxiety and depressive severity, with accuracy comparable to models using direct physiological sensing.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized data from consenting participants during naturalistic driving, including vehicle CAN-bus data and cabin audio, while using validated self-report measures (e.g., PANAS, PHQ-8) and, for a subset, wearable sensors (Empatica E4) as a ground-truth benchmark. We will engineer features from steering jerk, pedal entropy, and spectral/ prosodic audio features. A Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture, extending Picard's prior work, will model personalized temporal trajectories of affect, while deep learning models (e.g., LSTM networks) will capture sequential dependencies in the multi-modal signal stream.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for passive, indirect mental health assessment in a critical daily activity, moving beyond laboratory settings. It will establish the comparative efficacy of vehicle-derived signals versus physiological ones for specific affective constructs. The findings will directly inform the development of ethical, low-burden neurotech systems for early detection of mood disorders, contributing to responsible AI in health.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses direct sensing or single modalities, this proposal uniquely combines vehicular dynamics with vocal audio in a naturalistic context to infer a spectrum of clinically significant states (pain, anxiety, depression), explicitly testing if direct physiology is necessary for robust inference.

proposals.title: AffectStream: Continuous Affective Manifold Learning for Real-Time, Low-Power Embedded Vision
proposals.motivation: Current embedded affective vision systems rely on discrete emotion classification, which is computationally expensive, fails to capture the fluid nature of experience, and is ill-suited for tracking subtle, clinically relevant shifts in states like pain, anxiety, or depression. A continuous, low-dimensional model of affective flow is needed for always-on, resource-constrained applications in human-computer interaction, automotive driver monitoring, and mobile mental health sensing. This aligns with the need for personalized, continuous health prediction models and responsible, embedded neurotechnology.
proposals.hypothesis: That a continuous affective state can be modeled more efficiently and accurately for embedded vision by learning a low-dimensional manifold from visual features, compared to standard multi-class discrete emotion classifiers.
proposals.method: We will collect a multimodal dataset of facial video synchronized with self-reported affective valence/arousal and vocalizations. A lightweight MobileNetV3 backbone will extract visual features, which will be input to a temporal encoder (a small Transformer or GRU) to model dynamics. The core innovation is training this network to regress directly onto a continuous 2D affective space (valence-arousal) and to learn a smooth latent manifold via a variational autoencoder (VAE) regularization loss, encouraging efficient, fluid representations. The model will be optimized for deployment on embedded platforms like the NVIDIA Jetson Nano.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel, efficient architecture for continuous affective sensing on edge devices, 2) a publicly available dataset with continuous affective labels suitable for manifold learning, and 3) empirical evidence that continuous modeling outperforms discrete classification in both accuracy and computational efficiency for embedded vision tasks. It advances the field towards more nuanced, real-time tracking of mental states.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of continuous affective modeling (inspired by dimensional theories of emotion) with lightweight manifold learning specifically for embedded vision, replacing the dominant paradigm of discrete classification, is a novel and non-obvious systems approach.

proposals.title: Verifying Calm: A Multi-Modal Framework for Inducing and Validating Positive Low-Arousal States to Buffer Stress and Anxiety
proposals.motivation: Positive low-arousal affective states (e.g., calm, contentment) are theorized to be crucial for emotional regulation and resilience against depression and anxiety, yet they remain poorly defined and difficult to measure objectively. Pre-2026 affective computing and digital health research, including our own work on depression prediction, often focuses on high-arousal states or negative states, leaving a critical gap in creating tractable, evidence-based positive interventions. A rigorous framework for inducing and verifying these states is needed to move beyond self-report and build effective, personalized mental health technologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, guided induction techniques (e.g., slow-paced breathing combined with gratitude imagery) can reliably elicit a distinct positive low-arousal state, which will be characterized by a convergent multi-modal signature (physiological, vocal, and behavioral) and will functionally buffer participants' physiological and subjective stress responses to a subsequent mild laboratory stressor.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled study with participants (e.g., university students, a population of interest in our prior anxiety work) randomly assigned to different induction protocols. Physiological data (ECG, EDA, respiration) and short vocal samples will be collected continuously. We will employ Mixed Effects Random Forests (a method from our recent depression work) to model personalized physiological baselines and deviations during state induction. Vocal biomarkers (e.g., prosody, spectral tilt) will be analyzed to complement physiological markers. Post-induction, participants will undergo a standardized stress probe (e.g., a timed arithmetic task) to test the state's functional buffering effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, multi-method protocol for creating a key positive affective state in laboratory settings, moving the field from description to reliable induction. It will contribute a set of objective, convergent biomarkers for calm positive states, distinct from neutral rest or anhedonia. The findings will directly inform the development of more effective and verifiable digital interventions for anxiety and depression prevention, aligning with the goal of responsible and evidence-based neurotech.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated verification framework that pairs precise induction with objective multi-modal validation *and* a functional stress-buffering probe, ensuring the state is not just a physiological quietude but a functionally significant positive resource. This moves beyond correlational studies of naturally occurring calm to an experimental, causal paradigm for positive state science.

proposals.title: Incremental Random Forests for Longitudinal Modeling of Sustained Positive Affect from Continuous Wearable and Audio Data
proposals.motivation: Affective computing has focused heavily on detecting acute, high-arousal states like stress or joy, neglecting the foundational, low-arousal positive states (e.g., calm, serenity, engagement) that constitute psychological well-being. Characterizing the longitudinal 'texture' of these states from continuous, real-world data is critical for moving beyond episodic mental health assessment to understanding the dynamics of resilience and vulnerability. This aligns with the need for personalized, preventative mental health tools and responsible neurotech that respects the temporal nature of human experience.
proposals.hypothesis: That the statistical properties of sustained, low-arousal positive affect—its stability, rhythmicity, and contextual variability—can be reliably modeled from long-duration, multimodal behavioral data streams using online, incremental learning algorithms, and that these models will reveal novel predictors of longitudinal mental health outcomes like depression and anxiety.
proposals.method: We will develop an online, incremental Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) framework to model person-specific temporal trajectories of calm-positive states from fused data streams (e.g., EDA/HRV from wearables, prosodic features from lifelogging audio). The algorithm will incrementally update both fixed population-level and random individual-level effects as new data arrives. We will define the target 'calm-positive' construct using ecological momentary assessments of serenity and engagement, and engineer features capturing temporal 'texture' (e.g., autocorrelation, periodicity, entropy) over sliding windows of hours to days.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first computational framework for quantifying the longitudinal dynamics of sustained positive affect in naturalistic settings, moving affective science from event detection to baseline characterization. It will deliver a novel, open-source incremental MERF tool for the community and produce new insights into how the temporal patterns of calm states relate to depression and anxiety severity over time.
proposals.novelty: While MERFs have been used for static depression prediction, applying them in an online, incremental fashion to model the temporal structure of low-arousal positive states—rather than high-arousal negative ones—from unbounded data is a novel fusion of Picard's prior work on depression, vocalizations, and longitudinal modeling.

proposals.title: Robust Multimodal Fusion for Longitudinal Assessment of Pain and Depression in Daily Life
proposals.motivation: Current affective computing systems often rely on single-modality models (e.g., voice, EDA) that are brittle to real-world noise and fail to capture the complex, longitudinal nature of experiences like pain and depression. There is a critical need for robust, personalized models that can operate reliably outside controlled lab settings to support continuous mental and physical health monitoring. This aligns with the growing focus on digital phenotyping and responsible neurotech for real-world health applications.
proposals.hypothesis: A multimodal fusion architecture that jointly models physiological (e.g., EDA, ECG) and behavioral (e.g., vocal prosody, movement) signals with explicit temporal dynamics will yield more robust, generalizable, and interpretable predictions of affective states like pain and depressive severity than unimodal or late-fusion baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid deep learning architecture combining a Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) for personalized, interpretable longitudinal baselines with temporal neural networks (e.g., LSTMs or Transformers) for multimodal sequence modeling. Physiological data (from wrist-worn sensors) and behavioral features (from audio and accelerometry) will be processed through separate encoders, then fused via attention-based mechanisms. The model will be trained and validated on existing longitudinal datasets of student populations experiencing stress, anxiety, and self-reported pain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, robust computational framework for continuous affective state assessment that is less susceptible to sensor noise or missing modalities. It will advance the field of personalized health analytics by integrating interpretable longitudinal modeling with deep multimodal fusion, directly contributing to tools for monitoring depression and pain. The research will also yield insights into the temporal interplay of physiological and behavioral signals in real-world contexts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of MERFs—a method recently advanced by Picard's group for personalized depression prediction—with deep multimodal temporal fusion specifically for robustness, moving beyond standard late-fusion or ensemble approaches common in pre-2026 affective computing.

proposals.title: Beyond Heart Rate: Re-evaluating Red and Blue Photoplethysmography Channels for Multi-Modal Assessment of Pain and Affective States
proposals.motivation: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) for physiological sensing predominantly relies on the green channel, often discarding red and blue as noisy or useless for heart rate. However, these wavelengths have different absorption characteristics for blood constituents and penetrate tissue differently. Pre-2026 work in affective computing, especially by Picard's group on pain and depression, suggests a need for richer, multi-modal physiological markers beyond heart rate that are obtainable from ubiquitous cameras.
proposals.hypothesis: The red and blue channels in consumer-grade camera rPPG signals contain physiologically meaningful information, distinct from the green channel, that can be used to derive novel biomarkers correlated with self-reported pain intensity and transient affective states like anxiety.
proposals.method: We will collect synchronized facial video, audio (for vocal biomarkers), and self-report data (pain, anxiety) from a controlled lab study with students undergoing a validated cold-pressor task. Using a pre-2026 framework like EVM for motion-stabilized rPPG extraction, we will isolate independent component signals from red, green, and blue channels. We will then apply Mixed Effects Random Forests (a method from Picard's recent work) to model the relationship between novel features from red/blue channels (e.g., pulse shape morphology, low-frequency power ratios) and the self-reported outcomes, while accounting for individual differences.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the prevailing dismissal of red/blue rPPG channels and provide a new, low-cost method for extracting complementary physiological signals from standard video. It will contribute directly to the development of more robust, multi-modal digital biomarkers for pain and affective state assessment, aligning with Picard's focus on personalized health sensing and responsible neurotech.
proposals.novelty: While prior rPPG research focuses on optimizing green-channel heart rate, this proposal systematically investigates the discarded red and blue channels for a different purpose: extracting features predictive of pain and anxiety, not just cardiovascular metrics, by leveraging Picard's group expertise in affective modeling and personalization.

proposals.title: Assessing the Limits of Camera-Based Group Observations (CGO): A Study of Physiological Signal Recovery at Macro and Long-Range Distances
proposals.motivation: Camera-based Group Observations (CGO) systems, which use remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) and motion analysis for non-contact physiological and behavioral monitoring, are typically validated at standard conversational distances (1-3 meters). However, real-world health applications, such as monitoring pain in neonatal ICUs (requiring macro views) or assessing depression and anxiety in large lecture halls or public spaces (requiring long-range views), challenge this assumption. Understanding the system's performance at these extremes is critical for ethical and practical deployment in diverse clinical and educational settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the CGO system's performance will degrade non-linearly with distance, but that specific signal features (e.g., certain frequency bands in rPPG, or gross head movement patterns) will remain recoverable at macro and long-range distances using tailored computational methods, enabling novel applications in personalized health monitoring.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled lab study with student participants undergoing validated stress/pain induction tasks (e.g., cold pressor, Trier Social Stress Test). Simultaneous physiological ground truth (ECG, EDA) will be collected. Video will be captured using a high-resolution, calibrated camera system at varied distances: macro (<0.5m), standard (1-3m), and long-range (5-10m). We will adapt and test the Mixed Effects Random Forest (MERF) architecture from Picard's prior work, using it to model the relationship between recovered video features (e.g., rPPG signal-to-noise ratio, micro-movement spectral power) and ground-truth physiological states, with distance and individual as random effects. Deep learning models like EVM (Eulerian Video Magnification) will be used for signal amplification prior to feature extraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish practical boundaries and novel use cases for CGO technology, directly informing its responsible design for sensitive environments like NICUs and classrooms. It will contribute a new methodological framework for personalizing non-contact physiological models across varying observational constraints, advancing the field of affective computing towards more versatile and ethically-aware deployment.
proposals.novelty: While rPPG and behavioral sensing are established, systematically inverting the standard distance assumption to explore both macro and long-range extremes within a single personalized modeling framework is novel. This directly tackles socio-technical neuroethics challenges by empirically defining the physical limits of observational sensing, a prerequisite for building responsible neurotech.

proposals.title: Validating a Five-Band Camera for Physiological Sensing of Affective States During Naturalistic Movement
proposals.motivation: Affective computing and digital health tools, such as those for assessing pain, anxiety, and depression, increasingly rely on camera-based physiological sensing (e.g., remote photoplethysmography, rPPG). However, validation studies for novel multi-spectral cameras like the CGO often occur under constrained, static conditions, limiting their real-world applicability. For tools to be useful in daily life and clinical monitoring, they must perform reliably during the natural movements inherent to human experience.
proposals.hypothesis: The five-band CGO camera can extract robust physiological features (e.g., heart rate variability, pulse rate) correlated with self-reported affective states and stress, even under diverse, controlled motion conditions, with performance comparable to gold-standard wearable sensors.
proposals.method: We will recruit a student cohort (N=30) to perform a series of standardized movement tasks (e.g., typing, gesturing, walking in place) while undergoing a mild mental stressor (e.g., a timed arithmetic task). Physiological data will be captured simultaneously via the CGO camera and a reference ECG chest strap. We will employ established rPPG signal processing pipelines (e.g., CHROM, POS algorithms) and motion artifact reduction techniques. Extracted features will be used in mixed-effects random forest models to predict self-reported anxiety and stress levels, accounting for individual baselines and motion type as random effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a crucial validation dataset and performance benchmark for multi-spectral imaging in mobile affective sensing. It will directly inform the development of more robust, real-world tools for continuous mental health monitoring (e.g., for depression and anxiety) outside clinical settings. The findings will also contribute to responsible neurotech development by highlighting practical socio-technical challenges related to motion artifacts.
proposals.novelty: While rPPG motion robustness is studied for conventional RGB cameras, systematic validation of novel five-band cameras under diverse, ecologically valid motion conditions relevant to human-computer interaction is lacking. This study explicitly bridges the gap between controlled lab validation and the demands of real-world health sensing applications.

proposals.title: Feasibility of Multi-Spectral Imaging via Consumer Webcams for Continuous, Unobtrusive Assessment of Affective and Pain States
proposals.motivation: Continuous, passive monitoring of affective states like depression and anxiety, as well as acute pain, remains a significant challenge in digital health. Current methods often rely on self-report or specialized hardware, limiting scalability and ecological validity. The widespread availability of consumer-grade cameras presents a unique opportunity for accessible health sensing, but standard RGB imaging lacks the spectral specificity needed for robust physiological feature extraction linked to these states.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by implementing a computational five-band sensing approach using software-driven color filter array (CFA) modifications on standard consumer webcams, we can derive physiologically relevant signals (e.g., related to blood volume pulse and tissue oxygenation) sufficient to train personalized machine learning models for predicting self-reported depression severity and pain experience.
proposals.method: We will develop a software pipeline to emulate a five-band multispectral camera by leveraging the native CFA of common webcam sensors and applying tailored post-processing algorithms to extract narrowband spectral information. This virtual sensor data will be used to compute photoplethysmography (PPG) and pulse oximetry (SpO2) proxies. These features, along with vocal prosody and facial action unit data from the same video stream, will serve as input to Mixed Effects Random Forests (MERF), a method previously validated by our group, to model longitudinal changes in affect and pain within individuals during controlled lab studies with student participants.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for low-cost, software-based hyperspectral imaging on ubiquitous devices, directly advancing the field of affective computing. It will provide a novel methodological framework for integrating multi-modal physiological and behavioral data into personalized mental and physical health prediction models, moving towards more responsible and accessible neurotech.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in building new hardware, but in creating a purely computational method to transform commodity RGB cameras into multi-spectral health sensors, specifically tailored for longitudinal affective state modeling using MERFs, which account for individual differences—a key socio-technical challenge in neuroethics.

proposals.title: Multi-Person Remote Photoplethysmography for Group-Level Assessment of Affective and Physiological Synchrony
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in affective computing has established the value of camera-based physiological monitoring (e.g., heart rate, heart rate variability) for assessing individual states like pain, anxiety, and depression. However, many health-relevant experiences, such as social support, collaborative learning, and group therapy, are inherently interpersonal. Current methods are limited to single-subject analysis, missing the dyadic or group-level physiological dynamics that may underpin collective emotional experiences and mental health outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multi-task deep learning architecture, trained on synchronized five-band camera video and ground-truth physiological data from multiple individuals, can accurately recover individual vital signs and reveal novel, clinically relevant features of group-level physiological synchrony correlated with self-reported affective states.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-person rPPG pipeline using a five-band (RGB, NIR, 850nm, 940nm) camera. A YOLOv7-based detector will first localize and track faces. For each tracked region, we will extract spatial-temporal cubes and feed them into a shared-weight convolutional neural network with a Transformer-based temporal encoder, inspired by recent vision Transformer architectures for video analysis (pre-2026). This model will be trained via a multi-task loss function to simultaneously estimate heart rate and respiratory rate for each individual from the fused multi-spectral data. Validation will occur in controlled lab studies with small groups (e.g., 3-5 students) during collaborative tasks, using ECG chest straps and respiratory belts as ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated method for non-contact, simultaneous physiological monitoring of multiple individuals, enabling the study of group affective dynamics. It will contribute a new open-source dataset of multi-person synchronized physiology and affect, and offer preliminary evidence linking physiological synchrony features to self-reported group cohesion and individual anxiety during collaborative online learning scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While single-person remote physiological measurement is established, scaling to robust, simultaneous multi-person extraction using multi-spectral imaging and modern deep learning architectures for disentangling signals is novel. This moves the field from individual-centric to interaction-centric affective computing, aligning with Picard's recent focus on communities and responsible neurotech.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Modeling of Sustained Positive and Calm Affective States from Unbounded Behavioral Data
ideas.description: This research direction develops online, incremental algorithms to estimate the statistical 'texture' of low-arousal positive emotional states from very long-duration, continuous data streams, such as lifelogging video, audio, or physiological wearables. By focusing on calm and positive states, it shifts the technical challenge from detecting rare, high-arousal events to characterizing the persistent, baseline patterns of well-being. This enables the study of how states like serenity or engagement wax and wane over hours, days, or months in natural settings. The core innovation is a computational framework that defines and measures the stability, rhythmicity, and context-sensitive variations of positive affect at a timescale previously inaccessible to affective science.

ideas.title: Multi-Spectral In-Cabin Imaging for Passenger Emotional Biometrics and Ambient Adaptation
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel in-cabin sensing system that uses multi-spectral (including visible, near-infrared, and thermal) imaging to jointly model passenger emotional and physiological states. By moving beyond grayscale intensity and standard RGB, the system can detect nuanced biomarkers of emotion and comfort, such as subtle blood flow changes, localized thermal variations, and pupillary response under varying light conditions. This rich, multi-channel data is used to infer complex emotional states like anxiety, excitement, or discomfort, enabling the vehicle to automatically and proactively adjust ambient lighting, climate, audio, and visual displays to enhance passenger well-being and shared experience. The core innovation lies in using spectral fusion not for object recognition, but for passive, continuous emotional biometrics, creating a responsive cabin environment that adapts to the unspoken needs of its occupants.

ideas.title: Emotion-Aware Video Compression for In-Cabin Monitoring Using Temporal Texture Models
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel video compression framework for in-vehicle cabin monitoring that integrates the STAR (Spatio-Temporal Auto-Regressive) model for temporal textures with models of long-term emotional dynamics. Instead of compressing all visual data uniformly, the system learns and prioritizes the compression of spatio-temporal patterns (textures) that are predictive of emotional state transitions and long-term adaptation behaviors, such as growing fatigue or frustration. This enables ultra-efficient long-duration recording by discarding visually repetitive but emotionally irrelevant data, while preserving the fidelity needed for downstream analysis of emotional trajectories. The result is a dual-purpose technology that simultaneously solves a bandwidth/storage problem for automotive data loggers and provides a refined signal for studying human affective adaptation during extended human-machine interaction.

ideas.title: Real-Time Synthesis of Affective Auditory Textures from Indirect Driver Signals
ideas.description: This research develops a system that uses only indirect, non-invasive signals—such as steering wheel micro-movements, pedal inputs, and cabin audio (e.g., speech, sighs)—to infer a driver's cognitive-emotional state. This inferred state then drives a real-time temporal texture synthesis engine, which generates and continuously adapts a layer of ambient, non-distracting auditory feedback (e.g., calm rhythmic pulses, smooth tonal drones) within the vehicle cabin. The core innovation is the closed-loop, real-time synthesis of soundscapes that are dynamically textured to subtly counter detected stress or fatigue, moving beyond passive inference to active, ambient intervention. This enables a new form of in-vehicle human-computer interaction focused on implicit regulation, enhancing safety and comfort without the need for wearables or direct bio-sensing.

ideas.title: A Multi-Method Verification Framework for Inducing and Validating Specific Positive Low-Arousal Affective States
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond merely calling for the study of calm positive states by developing a rigorous experimental framework to reliably create and confirm them. It combines precise induction techniques (e.g., slow-paced breathing, specific guided imagery) with convergent, objective physiological and behavioral markers to verify the target state. A core innovation is the inclusion of post-induction functional probes to demonstrate the state's unique quality, such as its capacity to buffer stress or enhance subtle reward sensitivity. This approach ensures that studied states are scientifically tractable, distinguishable from neutral or pathological low-arousal, and linked to demonstrable functional outcomes.

ideas.title: Continuous Affective Flow for Embedded Vision
ideas.description: This research fuses a continuous model of emotion with lightweight embedded vision to create a system for real-time affective sensing. Instead of classifying discrete emotional states, it models emotion as a fluid trajectory within a learned, low-dimensional manifold directly from visual features. This approach is inherently more efficient for embedded devices, as it replaces complex multi-class classifiers with a simpler continuous regression or manifold mapping task. The result is a low-power vision system capable of tracking subtle, dynamic shifts in affective state, suitable for always-on applications in human-computer interaction, automotive safety, or wearable technology.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Uncertainty-Aware Multimodal Fusion with Anomaly Detection for Fault-Tolerant Emotion Recognition
ideas.description: This research develops a robust multimodal emotion recognition system designed to be resilient to sensor failures and adversarial noise. Instead of static fusion, it uses a dynamic gating mechanism that weights each modality (e.g., EEG, facial video, voice) based on real-time estimates of signal integrity and predictive uncertainty. The framework includes explicit modules for anomaly detection and cross-modal consistency validation, allowing it to identify and mitigate corrupted data streams. This ensures graceful degradation and maintains reliable performance even under partial system failure or targeted attacks on specific sensors.

ideas.title: STAR-Lite: A Self-Supervised, Lightweight Model for Real-Time Anomaly Detection in Embedded Vision
ideas.description: This research develops STAR-Lite, a drastically scaled-down Spatio-Temporal Auto-Regressive model designed for deployment on low-power embedded vision sensors. The core innovation is that the model is trained in a self-supervised manner on the device to learn the normal spatio-temporal texture and motion patterns of its specific deployment environment. By continuously comparing live input to its internal lightweight predictions, the system can detect anomalies as significant deviations, enabling real-time fault, intrusion, or unusual event detection. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive perception system that performs both recognition and novel anomaly detection within the severe computational constraints of edge devices.

ideas.title: Modeling Emotion as a Continuous Field Using Topological Data Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption of discrete emotional states by proposing to model emotion as a fluid, continuous construct. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where the structure of amorphous solids is analyzed as a continuous energy landscape, we adapt the solution of Topological Data Analysis (TDA). In that field, persistent homology maps the multi-scale topological shape of complex, continuous data without imposing discrete categories. Adapted to affective science, this approach would treat multimodal data streams (e.g., physiology, facial dynamics, voice) as points sampling a continuous emotional manifold. TDA would then be used to computationally characterize the intrinsic topology—the persistent 'holes,' 'loops,' and density variations—of this manifold. This reveals the natural continuous structure of emotional experience, identifying gradients, basins of attraction (metastable emotional states), and the fluid pathways between them, fundamentally moving beyond labeled categories to a dynamic field model of emotion.

ideas.title: Emotion as a Socially Constructed Signal in Traffic Systems
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that emotion in driving is an individual internal state. Drawing an analogy from collective animal behavior, where individual states like alarm are socially constructed signals that coordinate group movement, we adapt this solution to traffic research. We propose to model driver emotion as a dynamic, socially transmitted signal within the traffic network. A driver's behavior (e.g., aggressive maneuvering) acts as an emitted signal that is perceived by nearby road users, influencing their own behavioral and emotional signals. This creates feedback loops and cascading local patterns, making collective 'emotional tone' an emergent property of the traffic system itself, crucial for understanding risk propagation and flow breakdown.

ideas.title: A Continuous Emotional Manifold Grounded by Sparse, Interpretable Attractor Basins
ideas.description: This approach models emotion as a fluid continuum but introduces a sparse set of foundational 'attractor basins'—such as joy, anger, or sadness—anchored in cross-modal human data. These basins act as probabilistic regions within the manifold, providing stable, communicable reference points without enforcing discrete categorization. Any emotional state can be represented as a coordinate in the continuous space, with its description generated dynamically by its position and distance from these core attractors. This bridges the richness of a continuous model with the practical need for interpretable, actionable emotional intelligence.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Signal Denoising for Multi-Scale Texture Feature Extraction
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics, where wavelet transforms are used for the multi-scale denoising and feature isolation of seismic signals, to the problem of spatio-temporal texture analysis. In seismology, wavelets separate subsurface reflection patterns from noise across scales to interpret geological structures. By applying an analogous methodology—specifically, wavelet-based multi-resolution decomposition and scale-adaptive thresholding—to image or video data, we can isolate and quantitatively characterize dominant texture elements at multiple spatial and temporal scales. This adapted approach enables the robust extraction of salient texture features for applications like material classification, medical image analysis, or remote sensing, where distinguishing relevant multi-scale patterns from noise or heterogeneous backgrounds is critical.

ideas.title: Joint Learning of Latent Motion and Spatio-Temporal Representations
ideas.description: This approach integrates motion understanding directly into the model's architecture by learning a latent motion representation, rather than relying on precomputed optical flow. An auxiliary network branch learns to predict frame-to-frame displacements in a feature space, guided by a self-supervised reconstruction loss. These adaptive motion features are then fused with the main spatio-temporal pathway, enabling the entire system to be trained end-to-end. This results in motion cues that are robust, computationally efficient within the network, and specifically tuned for the end task's requirements.

ideas.title: ICA-HMM for State-Driven Source Separation in Temporal Textures
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a unified ICA-HMM framework to model temporal textures. The ICA component separates the observed mixed signal into a set of independent source textures, while the HMM concurrently models the discrete hidden states that govern the activation, weighting, or combination of these sources over time. This allows the model to not only identify the underlying source processes but also to learn the dynamic rules (state transitions) that dictate how these sources evolve and interact to generate the complex observed sequence. The result is a generative model capable of both parsing and synthesizing temporal textures with rich, state-dependent dynamics.

ideas.title: Biologically-Inspired Sparse Attention for Embedded Vision
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from computational neuroscience—specifically, the mammalian visual system's use of sparse, event-driven coding and selective attention—to the problem of scaling down vision transformers for embedded devices. The analogous biological system solves the problem of processing high-bandwidth sensory data with limited neural resources by extracting and transmitting only salient features (like edges and motion), ignoring redundant static information. The adapted idea, 'Biologically-Inspired Sparse Attention for Embedded Vision,' creates a lightweight STAR variant called 'Sparse-STAR.' It replaces the transformer's standard, computationally expensive dense self-attention with a gated, dynamic mechanism. This module identifies and activates attention only for the most informative spatial patches in a video frame (based on low-cost saliency cues like temporal difference or simple edge detection), forcing most tokens to attend to just a small, relevant subset. This mimics neural efficiency, creating a variable and significantly sparser computational graph. The result is a drastic reduction in FLOPs and memory bandwidth, enabling real-time visual recognition on resource-constrained embedded hardware while maintaining robust accuracy for dynamic scenes.

ideas.title: Spectral-Temporal Coherence Modeling for Ultra-High-Definition Dynamic Textures
ideas.description: This research direction develops novel estimation frameworks to model temporal textures in ultra-high-definition video sequences where the dynamics are expressed across multiple spectral channels (RGB, near-infrared, etc.) not just intensity. The core challenge is to efficiently scale the complex, joint spatio-spectral-temporal models required to capture subtle, wavelength-dependent dynamics like iridescence or material weathering. The resulting models would enable the synthesis and predictive editing of hyper-realistic, time-varying visual phenomena for applications in visual effects, digital heritage, and remote sensing, moving beyond mere pixel replication to capturing underlying photometric processes.

ideas.title: Adapting Ion Channel Gating Models to Infer Hidden States in Temporal Textures
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational neuroscience, where HMMs are used to infer the hidden conformational states of ion channels from noisy, time-series current data. The analogous problem in temporal texture analysis is modeling the hidden states that govern the visual evolution of dynamic textures (like flowing water or flickering fire). The adapted research idea treats the observed texture sequence as a macroscopic output driven by a finite set of hidden regimes. By training an HMM on temporal texture features, we can learn the transition dynamics between these hidden states, providing a generative model that captures the underlying stochastic process governing the texture's temporal evolution, much like modeling the gating dynamics underlying an ionic current.

ideas.title: Temporally-Aware Convolutive ICA with Learned Demixing for Complex Temporal Texture Separation
ideas.description: This approach first employs a neural network architecture to model the potentially convolutive and non-instantaneous mixing process inherent in temporal textures, transforming the data into a space where a linear mixing assumption is more valid. It then applies a modified ICA algorithm that uses a temporal contrast function, such as one maximizing the autocorrelation of separated components while minimizing cross-correlation, to better separate sources with dynamic structure. The system is trained end-to-end to jointly optimize the temporal demixing model and the source separation criterion, making it robust to the complex dependencies and delays found in real-world mixed signals like video textures or multi-sensor time-series.

ideas.title: Compressing Temporal Textures via Seismic Waveform Parameterization
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from geophysics. In seismology, repetitive seismic waveforms are compressed by fitting them to parametric models that describe the physical laws of wave propagation, storing only those parameters. Similarly, for video compression of temporal textures, we propose using the STAR model's parameters to capture the underlying generative process of the texture (e.g., dynamics, appearance statistics). Instead of compressing frame sequences, the encoder would fit the STAR model to the input video clip, extracting a compact parameter set. The decoder reconstructs the texture by running the generative model with these parameters. This approach targets high compression ratios for specific, hard-to-compress dynamic textures where traditional motion estimation fails.

ideas.title: Adaptive Reservoir Computing for Real-Time Temporal Texture Synthesis
ideas.description: This research proposes adapting the efficient temporal processing framework of Reservoir Computing (RC) from computational neuroscience to the problem of real-time synthesis of temporal textures. In RC, a fixed, randomly initialized recurrent 'reservoir' network generates complex temporal dynamics from an input signal, and only a simple linear output layer is trained—enabling fast learning and real-time operation for tasks like speech recognition. We adapt this solution by using a fixed reservoir network to generate a high-dimensional, evolving state from a simple control input. A lightweight, trainable readout layer (e.g., a small neural network or linear model) is then solely responsible for mapping these reservoir states to the visual output of the temporal texture. This architecture decouples the computationally expensive task of modeling long-term temporal dependencies (handled once, offline, by designing the reservoir) from the synthesis task, allowing for highly efficient training and execution suitable for real-time applications like video game effects, live visual performances, or interactive simulations.

ideas.title: Unsupervised Discovery of Nonlinear Regime-Shift Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction proposes developing a framework that combines nonlinear dynamical systems modeling (like neural ODEs or reservoir computing) with self-supervised learning objectives. The system would learn to reconstruct and forecast high-dimensional observational data, with the training process forcing the internal model to identify and disentangle latent dynamical regimes. The key innovation is that the nonlinear model's parameters or latent states become interpretable indicators of system phase, stability, and transition mechanisms, all discovered without supervised labels for these complex phenomena.

ideas.title: Wavelet-Conditioned Generative Models for Multi-Scale Spatio-Temporal Synthesis and Prediction
ideas.description: This approach integrates continuous wavelet transforms with deep generative networks to create a powerful framework for analysis, synthesis, and prediction. The system first decomposes spatio-temporal data (e.g., video, turbulent flows, medical imaging sequences) into multi-scale wavelet coefficients, capturing texture and structure explicitly. These coefficient maps then condition a generative model, such as a diffusion model, which learns the joint distribution of features across scales. This enables not only multi-scale analysis but also the generation of realistic future frames, the synthesis of coherent textures at any scale, and the identification of anomalies as deviations from the learned multi-scale priors.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Succession Models to Map the Dynamics of Low-Arousal Positive States
ideas.description: This research seed proposes expanding emotion science beyond high-arousal negative emotions to include positive and calm states. Drawing an analogy from ecology, where the field progressed by studying not just catastrophic disturbances but also the gradual process of ecological succession, this idea adapts that solution. The analogous solution involved modeling the predictable sequences and stable endpoints of ecosystem development. Adapted to psychology, the research would develop frameworks to map the dynamics, sequences, and sustaining conditions of low-arousal positive emotional states (e.g., contentment, tranquility). The goal is to understand these states not as mere absences of negativity, but as complex, structured 'emotional ecosystems' with their own developmental pathways, stability metrics, and functional contributions to long-term psychological health and resilience.

ideas.title: Dynamically Adaptive and Resource-Aware STAR for Robust Embedded Vision
ideas.description: This research creates a simplified, energy-efficient STAR model variant specifically for embedded vision systems. It integrates principles from nonlinear dynamical systems theory to analyze and characterize the stability and bifurcation boundaries of the model's parameters under varying computational budgets (e.g., clock speed, memory bandwidth) and input conditions. This theoretical foundation allows the design of a runtime controller that monitors the model's state and dynamically switches between pre-analyzed, stable operational modes—from a high-accuracy complex mode to several degraded but stable lightweight modes—to prevent catastrophic performance collapse. The result is a system that achieves a novel trade-off: it is both extremely resource-efficient and dynamically robust to the fluctuating, harsh operational environments typical of embedded platforms.

ideas.title: Task-Adaptive Sparse Feature Fields for Embedded Visual Recognition
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel embedded vision system that replaces fixed convolutional filters with a small set of learned, task-adaptive basis functions stored in a shared dictionary. At runtime, a lightweight selector module dynamically assembles a minimal, task-specific sparse feature field from this dictionary, drastically reducing the active parameter count and computational footprint for any given inference operation. This allows a single deployed model to efficiently switch between diverse recognition tasks like classification and segmentation by recombining its fundamental feature building blocks, rather than running multiple specialized networks. The result is a versatile, real-time perception system for edge devices that achieves multi-task capability within a stringent memory and power budget.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Wavelet Feature Distillation for Scalable and Discriminative Texture Recognition
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that leverages wavelet-based multi-scale spatio-temporal analysis to generate a rich, overcomplete feature set. To scale effectively to databases with thousands of texture classes, the core innovation is a feature distillation mechanism that learns to select and compress the most discriminative wavelet-derived features across scales for each texture family. This results in a compact, adaptive representation that maintains high accuracy on fine-grained distinctions while drastically reducing the computational and memory footprint compared to using full wavelet decompositions. The system thus achieves scalable recognition by intelligently filtering multi-scale information rather than processing it all, a synergy unattainable by either idea alone.

ideas.title: Conceptually Grounded Recognition via Human-Guided Self-Supervision
ideas.description: This research develops a recognition system that combines the scalability of self-supervised learning with targeted human guidance to ensure semantic validity. The core innovation is a training loop that intermittently queries a human oracle to evaluate and lightly annotate the conceptual coherence of clusters discovered by the model. These sparse, high-quality signals are used to define a regularization loss that aligns the model's latent space with human-understandable concepts. The result is a robust system that learns from predominantly unlabeled data while avoiding the pitfall of learning representations that are statistically sound but semantically meaningless for recognition tasks.

ideas.title: Causal Interaction Graphs for Disentangled Multi-Texture Dynamics
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework to model scenes with multiple dynamic textures by explicitly learning a sparse, directed graph that captures their causal interactions over time. The system disentangles each texture's intrinsic temporal evolution from the perturbation effects caused by other textures, using interventions derived from data augmentation to identify the interaction structure. This approach allows for robust prediction, generation, and control in multi-texture scenes, generalizing to unseen combinations and interaction strengths. The resulting models provide interpretable insights into the compositional dynamics of complex visual phenomena.

ideas.title: Optical Flow-Informed Hidden Markov Models for Physics-Aware Temporal Texture Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction fuses Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) with dense optical flow preprocessing to create a novel framework for analyzing temporal textures. The optical flow fields are not used as simple inputs but are quantized into discrete, interpretable motion primitives (e.g., direction and magnitude bins) which serve as the observable emissions for the HMM. The hidden states of the HMM thus probabilistically represent the latent, governing physical regimes—such as different phases of fluid flow or material deformation—that are responsible for generating the observed motion patterns. This synergy creates a powerful, generative model that can both segment videos into distinct physical-behavior phases and synthesize realistic, physics-consistent texture dynamics by sampling from the learned state-transition and emission models.

ideas.title: Spectral Disentanglement for Photometric Invariance in Multi-Channel Vision
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple joint color modeling to explicitly separate the intrinsic spectral reflectance of materials from extrinsic illumination and sensor effects. A neural network is trained, potentially with physics-based constraints, to reconstruct a scene's hyperspectral profile or robust multi-spectral features from standard RGB or limited-channel inputs. The system is adversarially trained with heavy augmentations that simulate real-world spectral variations, ensuring the learned representations are invariant to photometric confounding factors. This results in a model that generalizes robustly across different cameras, lighting conditions, and can even infer information beyond the visible spectrum.

ideas.title: Hierarchical and Adaptive Recognition for Scalable Embedded Vision
ideas.description: This research fuses the goals of scaling to massive databases and operating on low-power embedded devices by developing a hierarchical, adaptive variant of the STAR framework. The core innovation is a multi-tier recognition system where a lightweight front-end on the embedded device performs fast, coarse filtering using compressed descriptors, querying only relevant subsets of a massive cloud-based database. The system adapts its computational footprint in real-time, using simpler models for common textures and activating more complex, accurate models only for ambiguous or novel inputs. This creates a synergistic embedded-cloud pipeline that delivers large-database accuracy with embedded-device power constraints, enabling new applications in field robotics and IoT sensing.

ideas.title: Seismic Anomaly Detection via Spatiotemporal Texture Deviation
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from geophysics, where the ETAS model is used to detect anomalous seismic activity against a background model. Here, we adapt that solution to the problem of anomaly detection in dynamic visual textures. We propose to model the normal, dynamic texture of a scene (its 'visual seismicity') using a Spatiotemporal AutoRegressive (STAR) model. Significant deviations from this learned model, calculated over local 3D space-time patches, will signal anomalies. This transforms the texture anomaly detection problem into a familiar statistical deviation detection task, leveraging robust techniques from another field to identify irregularities in scaling and dynamics.

ideas.title: Adapting Non-Local Means Filtering to Extend STAR Models for Bidirectional Dependencies
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to a parallel problem in image processing—specifically, the Non-Local Means denoising algorithm—to extend STAR models. In NLM, denoising is performed using a weighted average from all image pixels, with weights determined by the similarity of local patches, creating effective 'non-local' neighborhoods. We propose to adapt this core idea to spatio-temporal statistical modeling by redefining the neighborhood structure in STAR models. The new model would replace fixed causal lags with a data-driven, similarity-based weighting scheme. A neighborhood for a target spatio-temporal point would include all other points (past, present, or future within the observational window) whose local spatio-temporal 'pattern patch' is similar, as measured by a kernel function. This allows the model to incorporate information bidirectionally in time and non-locally in space, better capturing complex dependencies like synchronous oscillations or lagged teleconnections that are not confined to adjacent or strictly past points.

ideas.title: Replace STAR with a Neural ODE for Complex Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea challenges the linearity assumption in regime-switching models by drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience. In that field, the complex, non-linear dynamics of neuron spiking were successfully modeled by moving from linear approximations to systems of nonlinear ODEs (e.g., Hodgkin-Huxley). Adapting this solution, we propose to replace the linear STAR model with a Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural ODE) framework. The core temporal evolution of the model's hidden state is governed by a neural network-parameterized, nonlinear ODE. This allows the model to learn and represent far more complex dynamic patterns, smooth or abrupt transitions, and long-range dependencies in time series data, moving beyond the limitations of linear or piecewise-linear transition functions.

ideas.title: Model color channels with multispectral unmixing techniques
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that color channels should be processed independently or as a simple stacked vector. By drawing an analogy from remote sensing, where hyperspectral unmixing decomposes a pixel's spectrum into constituent material signatures, we propose to model the color data in standard images similarly. The adapted solution involves treating an RGB or multi-spectral pixel's measurement as a linear (or non-linear) mixture of a small set of fundamental 'color endmembers,' which could represent intrinsic material colors, distinct light sources, or shadow components. Applying unmixing algorithms would allow for the joint, model-based interpretation of color channels, potentially leading to more robust feature representation by explicitly factoring out confounding variables like illumination and improving tasks like material segmentation, intrinsic image decomposition, and photometric invariance.

ideas.title: A Multi-Agent System Approach to Modeling Interacting Temporal Textures
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecology for modeling multi-species ecosystems to the challenge of modeling scenes with multiple interacting temporal textures. In ecology, systems like predator-prey dynamics are solved using frameworks that define both the intrinsic dynamics of individual species and the mathematical terms for their interactions. Adapted to computer vision and graphics, each temporal texture in a scene is modeled as an autonomous agent with its own generative statistical model. The research then focuses on defining and learning the interaction rules or coupling functions between these texture agents—such as competition, blending, or statistical transfer at boundaries—enabling the synthesis and analysis of complex, dynamic scenes where textures do not simply coexist but actively influence each other's evolution and appearance over time.
